meta.title: Displacement Governance Diagnostic
meta.editorialNotes: Primary analytical coding completed for all 40 cases. Continuity ratings and political-economy fields are expert analytical judgments and should remain visibly distinguished from documented formal design.

primaryFaultLines.label: Categorical
primaryFaultLines.definition: Protection changes or ends when a person is reclassified across legal, administrative or programme categories.

primaryFaultLines.label: Temporal
primaryFaultLines.definition: Protection changes or ends when an emergency, project, programme, mandate or funding period expires before displacement-related vulnerability resolves.

primaryFaultLines.label: Territorial
primaryFaultLines.definition: Protection changes or ends when a person crosses a border, district, municipality, provider catchment or other jurisdictional boundary.

sustainableInclusionDomains.label: Systems integration
sustainableInclusionDomains.definition: Embedding displaced or mobile populations in ordinary public systems, service pathways, registries and administrative procedures.

sustainableInclusionDomains.label: Fiscal integration
sustainableInclusionDomains.definition: Embedding displacement-related obligations in durable, recurrent or transferable public financing arrangements.

sustainableInclusionDomains.label: Sustained institutional responsibility
sustainableInclusionDomains.definition: Maintaining clear responsibility, authority and accountability across institutions, jurisdictions and displacement phases.

cases.title: AALCO Bangkok Principles

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Bangkok Principles are AALCO's regional principles on the status and treatment of refugees. Originally adopted in 1966 and revised in 2001, they are one of the few regional soft-law instruments in Asia and Africa dealing expressly with refugee status, treatment, asylum, non-refoulement, burden sharing, and durable solutions.
responsibleInstitutions: Regional legal framing rests with AALCO member states, but implementation depends on national ministries, immigration authorities, asylum or refugee bodies where they exist, courts, public service agencies, and cooperation with UNHCR or humanitarian actors.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility is framed through refugee and asylum categories rather than vulnerability, movement history, public service need, or displacement-related risk across the full continuum.
policyRecord.financing: The principles do not create a financing channel. Any costs must be absorbed through national budgets, humanitarian funding, host community support, or separate burden-sharing arrangements.
policyRecord.dataSystems: No direct registry or interoperable data system is created. Documentation depends on national asylum or immigration systems, UNHCR registration, or ad hoc administrative arrangements.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: No direct delivery system is established. Inclusion requires translation into immigration, civil registration, social protection, health, education, labour, or local government systems.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is weak unless national documents, recognition decisions, or assistance records are accepted across borders or jurisdictions.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is primarily diplomatic and normative. The principles do not create a direct complaints mechanism, enforceable entitlement, or operational accountability pathway for affected people.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term regional normative framework, with operational effects only where states incorporate, reference, or apply it in domestic practice.

displacementTransition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: People who fit refugee or asylum-related categories as understood through regional and international protection norms may be recognised analytically by the instrument. The framework is most relevant to cross-border displacement involving persecution, conflict, or serious protection risk.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when regional principles are not translated into domestic legal status, civil registration, budget lines, health coverage, education access, work authorization, or portable documentation. Protection remains normatively acknowledged but institutionally thin.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when regional principles are not translated into domestic legal status, civil registration, budget lines, health coverage, education access, work authorization, or portable documentation. Protection remains normatively acknowledged but institutionally thin.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Refugee recognition is acknowledged normatively but remains subject to domestic state discretion.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Regional principles do not assign operational authority to a domestic delivery institution.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No automatic fiscal obligation or financing channel is created.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Regional principles create a shared protection vocabulary while preserving state discretion over recognition, services, and fiscal responsibility.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People displaced by disasters, development projects, climate stress, statelessness, mixed migration, internal movement, or administrative exclusion are not automatically covered unless national systems or other legal regimes separately recognise them.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Bangkok Principles create a regional vocabulary for refugee protection, but they do not by themselves carry displaced people into national registration, financing, or service systems.
analysis.governanceFunction: The principles provide a sovereignty-preserving normative framework. They allow states to discuss refugee protection in regional terms without requiring accession to the 1951 Refugee Convention or creating automatic domestic entitlements. Their governance function is therefore classificatory and diplomatic rather than administrative or fiscal.
analysis.included: People who fit refugee or asylum-related categories as understood through regional and international protection norms may be recognised analytically by the instrument. The framework is most relevant to cross-border displacement involving persecution, conflict, or serious protection risk.
analysis.excluded: People displaced by disasters, development projects, climate stress, statelessness, mixed migration, internal movement, or administrative exclusion are not automatically covered unless national systems or other legal regimes separately recognise them.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when regional principles are not translated into domestic legal status, civil registration, budget lines, health coverage, education access, work authorization, or portable documentation. Protection remains normatively acknowledged but institutionally thin.
analysis.whyItMatters: The Bangkok Principles illustrate the difference between normative protection architecture and operational inclusion. They are important for understanding Asian and African regional approaches to refugee protection, but a governance map must ask whether such principles connect to systems people actually use. The political economy archetype is sovereignty-preserving ambiguity: states accept a shared vocabulary while retaining discretion over recognition, services, and fiscal responsibility.

cases.title: Guiding Principles on Internal Displacement

populationGroups: People at risk of internal displacement

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Guiding Principles on Internal Displacement set out rights and guarantees relevant to the protection of internally displaced persons before, during, and after displacement. They are not a treaty, but they have become the central international reference point for IDP law, policy, and institutional responsibility.
responsibleInstitutions: Primary responsibility rests with national authorities. International humanitarian and development actors support implementation but do not replace sovereign responsibility.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility turns on internal displacement status, coercive movement, displacement-related risk, and remaining within national borders. Domestic laws may further specify categories and procedures.
policyRecord.financing: The principles do not create budget lines. Financing depends on national appropriations, local government resources, humanitarian appeals, development finance, and recovery programmes.
policyRecord.dataSystems: No registry is created by the principles. Data architecture depends on national IDP registries, civil registration, disaster systems, humanitarian assessments, or sectoral databases.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery depends on ordinary public systems, humanitarian assistance, local authorities, and sectoral ministries. The principles themselves are not a delivery mechanism.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is essential but not guaranteed. Entitlements and records must follow IDPs across districts, camps, host communities, return areas, and settlement locations.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on domestic law, courts, administrative grievance mechanisms, national human rights institutions, international monitoring, and political oversight.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Full displacement cycle: prevention, protection during displacement, humanitarian assistance, durable solutions, return, resettlement, and reintegration.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: People forced or obliged to flee or leave their homes or places of habitual residence, particularly as a result of armed conflict, generalized violence, human rights violations, or disasters, who have not crossed an internationally recognized state border.

People forced or obliged to flee or leave their homes or places of habitual residence, particularly as a result of armed conflict, generalized violence, human rights violations, or disasters, who have not crossed an internationally recognized state border.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when IDP recognition is not connected to civil registration, housing, land and property systems, social protection, education, health care, local government finance, durable solutions planning, or post-return accountability.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when IDP recognition is not connected to civil registration, housing, land and property systems, social protection, education, health care, local government finance, durable solutions planning, or post-return accountability.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: IDP membership is defined broadly in normative terms but depends on domestic recognition and implementation.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: National responsibility is affirmed, but operational mandates must be created domestically.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No budget line or financing mechanism follows from the principles themselves.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The principles define national responsibility for IDPs but do not themselves create budgets, delivery systems, registries, or enforceable service entitlements.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People who cross an international border become subject to other legal and institutional regimes. People affected by slower-onset development, climate, livelihood, or administrative displacement may be left out when national systems interpret internal displacement narrowly.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Guiding Principles define IDP rights across displacement phases, but protection continuity depends on whether states translate them into budgets, mandates, data systems, and ordinary services.
analysis.governanceFunction: The principles convert internal displacement into a matter of national responsibility, supported by international law by analogy and human rights standards. Their function is to clarify what protection should cover, while leaving implementation to domestic governance systems and international support arrangements.
analysis.included: People forced or obliged to flee or leave their homes or places of habitual residence, particularly as a result of armed conflict, generalized violence, human rights violations, or disasters, who have not crossed an internationally recognized state border.
analysis.excluded: People who cross an international border become subject to other legal and institutional regimes. People affected by slower-onset development, climate, livelihood, or administrative displacement may be left out when national systems interpret internal displacement narrowly.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when IDP recognition is not connected to civil registration, housing, land and property systems, social protection, education, health care, local government finance, durable solutions planning, or post-return accountability.
analysis.whyItMatters: The Guiding Principles are foundational for IDP governance, but they do not themselves create an implementation architecture. The governance problem is whether national systems can carry responsibility across prevention, emergency response, protracted displacement, return, local integration, and settlement elsewhere. The political economy archetype is normative responsibility without automatic fiscal absorption.

cases.title: UN Secretary-General Action Agenda on Internal Displacement

policyRecord.whatItIs: The UN Secretary-General's Action Agenda on Internal Displacement sets out a system-wide vision to better resolve, prevent, and address internal displacement. It followed the High-Level Panel on Internal Displacement and places particular emphasis on durable solutions, prevention, data, development engagement, and national ownership.
responsibleInstitutions: National governments retain primary responsibility. The UN system, development actors, humanitarian agencies, financial institutions, and local authorities are expected to align behind nationally owned prevention and solutions efforts.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends less on a single entitlement rule and more on whether IDPs and affected communities are recognised in national solutions strategies, development plans, local plans, and data systems.
policyRecord.financing: The agenda calls for more predictable development and solutions financing, but does not itself create a financing instrument. Financing must be mobilised through national budgets, development banks, UN programmes, donors, and local planning systems.
policyRecord.dataSystems: The agenda emphasizes better IDP data, national statistics, and evidence for solutions. Interoperability with civil registration, social protection, local planning, and sectoral data remains the implementation challenge.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery depends on national and local institutions, UN country teams, humanitarian operations, development programmes, and sectoral public systems.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is a core requirement for solutions: IDPs need documentation, services, land or housing claims, social protection access, and livelihoods support that survive movement and return.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on national leadership, UN system coordination, solutions strategies, monitoring, and follow-up mechanisms. Direct entitlements require domestic translation.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Medium- to long-term, spanning immediate response, protracted displacement, prevention, durable solutions, and development planning.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

mechanisms.scope: IDPs in emergency and protracted situations, host communities, people seeking return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, and populations at risk of future displacement.
IDPs in emergency and protracted situations, host communities, people seeking return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, and populations at risk of future displacement.
IDPs in emergency and protracted situations, host communities, people seeking return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, and populations at risk of future displacement.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when emergency assistance does not transition into development planning, when local authorities lack resources, when IDP data are not integrated into national statistics, or when solutions strategies lack budget authority.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when emergency assistance does not transition into development planning, when local authorities lack resources, when IDP data are not integrated into national statistics, or when solutions strategies lack budget authority.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: IDPs and host communities are framed as development and solutions constituencies rather than only humanitarian caseloads.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The agenda promotes national ownership and UN-system alignment but lacks direct implementation authority.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: It seeks development and solutions finance but does not itself create recurrent national expenditure obligations.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The agenda seeks to move internal displacement from humanitarian management into national planning, development finance, prevention, and solutions systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People may remain outside if national plans do not recognise them, if displacement data are weak, if solutions are defined narrowly as return, or if financing remains humanitarian and short-term.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Action Agenda shifts internal displacement from humanitarian management toward development, prevention, and solutions, but success depends on whether states and partners embed IDP inclusion in ordinary planning and financing systems.
analysis.governanceFunction: The Action Agenda reframes internal displacement as a cross-cutting governance and development issue rather than a residual humanitarian caseload. It seeks to align national responsibility, UN system support, development financing, prevention, and solutions planning.
analysis.included: IDPs in emergency and protracted situations, host communities, people seeking return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, and populations at risk of future displacement.
analysis.excluded: People may remain outside if national plans do not recognise them, if displacement data are weak, if solutions are defined narrowly as return, or if financing remains humanitarian and short-term.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when emergency assistance does not transition into development planning, when local authorities lack resources, when IDP data are not integrated into national statistics, or when solutions strategies lack budget authority.
analysis.whyItMatters: The Action Agenda is central to the Mapping because it articulates the move from humanitarian response to governance architecture. It asks whether internal displacement can be absorbed into ordinary national systems, local development, prevention, and financing. The political economy archetype is development-system absorption under conditions of national ownership and donor alignment.

cases.title: Global Compact on Refugees

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Global Compact on Refugees is a non-binding international framework affirmed by the UN General Assembly in 2018. It aims to strengthen responsibility sharing, ease pressure on host countries, enhance refugee self-reliance, expand third-country solutions, and support conditions for voluntary return.
responsibleInstitutions: Host states retain responsibility for domestic systems and legal access. Donors, development banks, UNHCR, UN agencies, NGOs, private actors, and refugees themselves are expected to contribute through the compact's responsibility-sharing architecture.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility follows refugee status and host-country rules, with inclusion often mediated by registration, documentation, location, sectoral policy, and national programme criteria.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is mobilised through donor pledges, development cooperation, concessional finance, humanitarian assistance, host government budgets, and multi-stakeholder support platforms.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Refugee registration, host country administrative data, sectoral systems, social registries, education and health data, and development monitoring systems all shape implementation.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery may occur through national services, UNHCR-supported systems, humanitarian partners, development programmes, municipalities, schools, health facilities, and social protection programmes.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on documentation, legal status, movement rules, recognition across jurisdictions, and access to services outside camps or assigned locations.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is political and programmatic rather than judicial. Global Refugee Forums, pledges, indicators, host-state policy, donor reporting, and protection monitoring shape follow-up.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Medium- to long-term framework for protracted displacement, inclusion, solutions, and international cooperation.

displacementTransition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

mechanisms.scope: Refugees and host communities are the core populations. Returnees and stateless persons may be included where relevant to a particular situation or pledge.
Refugees and host communities are the core populations. Returnees and stateless persons may be included where relevant to a particular situation or pledge.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when pledges do not translate into budget support, when refugee inclusion remains project-based, when host states restrict legal status or work rights, or when national systems lack capacity to absorb additional users.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when pledges do not translate into budget support, when refugee inclusion remains project-based, when host states restrict legal status or work rights, or when national systems lack capacity to absorb additional users.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Refugee and host-community inclusion remains mediated by host-state policy and refugee status.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The compact coordinates voluntary responsibility sharing without transferring sovereign implementation authority.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: External support can enable system expansion but remains pledge- and programme-dependent.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugee inclusion becomes more feasible where host-state policy, international financing, donor commitments, and development cooperation align.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: IDPs, migrants, disaster-displaced people, and development-displaced populations are outside the compact's central refugee frame unless addressed through related national or international mechanisms.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Global Compact on Refugees promotes inclusion in national systems and more predictable responsibility sharing, but implementation depends on host-state choices, donor financing, and development-system alignment.
analysis.governanceFunction: The compact links refugee protection to development cooperation, national systems, host community support, financing, and multi-stakeholder pledging. Its function is to shift refugee response away from purely parallel humanitarian assistance toward more predictable inclusion and burden sharing.
analysis.included: Refugees and host communities are the core populations. Returnees and stateless persons may be included where relevant to a particular situation or pledge.
analysis.excluded: IDPs, migrants, disaster-displaced people, and development-displaced populations are outside the compact's central refugee frame unless addressed through related national or international mechanisms.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when pledges do not translate into budget support, when refugee inclusion remains project-based, when host states restrict legal status or work rights, or when national systems lack capacity to absorb additional users.
analysis.whyItMatters: The compact is a key global instrument for sustainable inclusion because it legitimises refugee access to ordinary systems while recognising host-country costs. The political economy archetype is conditional inclusion through responsibility sharing: host states may include refugees when international financing, development cooperation, and political incentives align.

cases.title: Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration

populationGroups: Families and communities affected by migration

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration is a non-binding intergovernmental agreement adopted in 2018. It provides a cooperative framework for migration governance across objectives including data, regular pathways, decent work, services, identity, border governance, return, reintegration, and international cooperation.
responsibleInstitutions: States carry primary implementation responsibility. UN Network on Migration, IOM, OHCHR, ILO, UNHCR where relevant, civil society, employers, local governments, and diaspora actors support implementation.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on migrant status, national law, documentation, sectoral rules, and programme criteria rather than a single global entitlement.
policyRecord.financing: The compact does not create a direct financing entitlement. Implementation depends on national budgets, development cooperation, diaspora and remittance systems, labour market institutions, and international partnerships.
policyRecord.dataSystems: The compact emphasizes better migration data, identity documentation, evidence-based policymaking, and international data cooperation. It does not create a single registry.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery occurs through national migration systems, consular services, labour institutions, local governments, education and health systems, border authorities, and service providers.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on recognition of identity documents, qualifications, social security arrangements, remittance channels, labour agreements, and return/reintegration systems.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is voluntary and review-based, including national implementation, regional review, the International Migration Review Forum, and stakeholder reporting.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term cooperative framework for international migration governance and implementation of the 23 objectives.

displacementTransition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: Migrants in international migration contexts, including migrant workers, people in vulnerable situations, families, and communities affected by migration.
Migrants in international migration contexts, including migrant workers, people in vulnerable situations, families, and communities affected by migration.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when migrants move between regular and irregular status, when service access depends on documentation, when labour protections do not follow across borders, or when return and reintegration are not connected to social protection and local development systems.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when migrants move between regular and irregular status, when service access depends on documentation, when labour protections do not follow across borders, or when return and reintegration are not connected to social protection and local development systems.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Migrants are recognized through a cooperative framework while admission and status remain sovereign.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Implementation is dispersed across domestic migration, labour and service institutions.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No automatic financing obligation is created.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The compact promotes rights-based migration cooperation while preserving state control over admission, status, and national implementation.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Refugees remain governed by separate refugee protection frameworks. IDPs and people displaced internally by conflict, disasters, development projects, or climate stress are not the compact's primary population unless migration pathways or cross-border mobility are implicated.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Global Compact for Migration promotes rights-based and cooperative migration governance, but it separates migrants from refugees and leaves access to services, status, and protection largely dependent on domestic systems.
analysis.governanceFunction: The compact organises migration as a whole-of-government and whole-of-society governance issue. It creates a policy vocabulary for cooperation while preserving state sovereignty over admission, status, and national implementation.
analysis.included: Migrants in international migration contexts, including migrant workers, people in vulnerable situations, families, and communities affected by migration.
analysis.excluded: Refugees remain governed by separate refugee protection frameworks. IDPs and people displaced internally by conflict, disasters, development projects, or climate stress are not the compact's primary population unless migration pathways or cross-border mobility are implicated.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when migrants move between regular and irregular status, when service access depends on documentation, when labour protections do not follow across borders, or when return and reintegration are not connected to social protection and local development systems.
analysis.whyItMatters: The compact is important for displacement governance because many displaced people are administratively treated as migrants when they move outside refugee or IDP categories. The political economy archetype is managed mobility with rights language: cooperation expands, but domestic status control remains central.

cases.title: World Bank ESS5

policyRecord.whatItIs: Environmental and Social Standard 5 is the World Bank standard on land acquisition, restrictions on land use, and involuntary resettlement under the Environmental and Social Framework. It applies to Investment Project Financing and sets borrower obligations for avoiding, minimizing, compensating, and managing project-related displacement impacts.
responsibleInstitutions: Borrowers implement project obligations under World Bank requirements, with Bank oversight through preparation, appraisal, supervision, environmental and social commitments, and implementation support.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility is tied to project impact, cut-off dates, affected-person status, land use, livelihood loss, and resettlement planning categories.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is project-bounded. Mitigation, compensation, resettlement, livelihood restoration, monitoring, and grievance costs are attached to the financed operation and borrower commitments.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Project census, socio-economic surveys, asset inventories, resettlement action plans, livelihood restoration plans, grievance records, and monitoring systems form the core data architecture.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through project implementation units, borrower agencies, contractors, local authorities, compensation mechanisms, livelihood restoration programmes, and grievance mechanisms.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited unless project records and entitlements connect affected people to permanent land, housing, income, civil registration, public services, and social protection systems.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes borrower grievance mechanisms, World Bank supervision, environmental and social commitment plans, disclosure requirements, and potential recourse through the World Bank Accountability Mechanism.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Project lifecycle, with residual obligations where livelihood restoration and monitoring continue after displacement occurs.

displacementTransition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People physically or economically displaced by World Bank-financed project activities, including some people without formal land title, may fall within the standard's protection framework.
People physically or economically displaced by World Bank-financed project activities, including some people without formal land title, may fall within the standard's protection framework.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when compensation or resettlement action plans are treated as project deliverables rather than bridges into ordinary housing, livelihood, land administration, civil registration, social protection, and service systems.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when compensation or resettlement action plans are treated as project deliverables rather than bridges into ordinary housing, livelihood, land administration, civil registration, social protection, and service systems.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility is bounded to project-affected-person classifications and cut-off rules.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Borrower and project responsibilities are strong during the project but weaken at closure unless transferred.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Costs are project-bounded and designed to close rather than become recurrent public liabilities.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Displacement is governed as a project-related environmental and social risk, with obligations bounded by project scope, financing, and supervision.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People affected indirectly, downstream, cumulatively, across borders, after project closure, or outside the financed project's defined area may be harder to capture unless the project assessment includes them.

analysis.oneLineFinding: ESS5 governs displacement created by World Bank-financed projects, but its protections can end at the project boundary before livelihood, service, and territorial continuity are restored.
analysis.governanceFunction: ESS5 translates development finance into procedural and substantive obligations for resettlement, compensation, livelihood restoration, consultation, grievance handling, and mitigation of project-induced displacement.
analysis.included: People physically or economically displaced by World Bank-financed project activities, including some people without formal land title, may fall within the standard's protection framework.
analysis.excluded: People affected indirectly, downstream, cumulatively, across borders, after project closure, or outside the financed project's defined area may be harder to capture unless the project assessment includes them.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when compensation or resettlement action plans are treated as project deliverables rather than bridges into ordinary housing, livelihood, land administration, civil registration, social protection, and service systems.
analysis.whyItMatters: ESS5 is central to displacement governance because it shows how development finance creates obligations, while also showing how project logic can narrow responsibility by time, territory, and causation. The political economy archetype is safeguard-based mitigation: displacement is governed as project risk rather than as long-term inclusion.

cases.title: IFC Performance Standard 5

populationGroups: People physically displaced by private-sector projects
People economically displaced by private-sector projects

policyRecord.whatItIs: IFC Performance Standard 5 is the IFC standard on land acquisition and involuntary resettlement. It forms part of IFC's Sustainability Framework and applies to IFC clients whose projects involve land acquisition, restrictions on land use, physical displacement, or economic displacement.
responsibleInstitutions: The IFC client carries implementation responsibility, with IFC review and supervision. Government authorities may also be involved in land acquisition, permits, compensation, and local service systems.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility is tied to project impact, displacement category, land or asset use, cut-off dates, and client assessment of affected persons and communities.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is project and client-bounded. Costs are carried through project budgets, compensation frameworks, resettlement action plans, livelihood restoration plans, and lender-required mitigation measures.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Socio-economic baseline surveys, asset inventories, resettlement action plans, livelihood restoration plans, stakeholder engagement records, grievance logs, and monitoring reports form the data architecture.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery occurs through client systems, consultants, local authorities, contractors, compensation mechanisms, livelihood programmes, and grievance processes.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is weak unless project records connect affected people to public documentation, land systems, social protection, livelihood services, and long-term local development planning.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes client grievance mechanisms, IFC supervision, lender requirements, disclosure expectations, and potential complaints to the Compliance Advisor Ombudsman where applicable.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Project and investment lifecycle, with ongoing monitoring where livelihood restoration or displacement impacts continue.

displacementTransition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People and communities physically or economically displaced by IFC-supported client activities, including people with formal rights, customary claims, or recognized use of land and assets, depending on the circumstances.

People and communities physically or economically displaced by IFC-supported client activities, including people with formal rights, customary claims, or recognized use of land and assets, depending on the circumstances.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when livelihood restoration is time-limited, when company-managed processes are not connected to public land administration or social services, or when accountability weakens after financial close or project completion.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when livelihood restoration is time-limited, when company-managed processes are not connected to public land administration or social services, or when accountability weakens after financial close or project completion.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility is tied to private-project impact and client assessment.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Responsibility rests with the client under lender supervision rather than ordinary public systems.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Mitigation costs are internalized within the project and investment lifecycle.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Private-sector displacement risk is internalized through client obligations, lender requirements, mitigation plans, and project accountability systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People indirectly affected through cumulative impacts, market shifts, downstream effects, informal tenure not recognised in practice, or impacts beyond the project area may be excluded if the client's assessment does not capture them.

analysis.oneLineFinding: IFC Performance Standard 5 extends displacement governance into private-sector finance, but responsibility remains anchored to client risk management and project boundaries rather than public-system inclusion.
analysis.governanceFunction: The standard turns displacement risk into a condition of private-sector finance. It requires clients to avoid or minimize displacement, compensate affected people, restore livelihoods, engage communities, and establish grievance mechanisms.
analysis.included: People and communities physically or economically displaced by IFC-supported client activities, including people with formal rights, customary claims, or recognized use of land and assets, depending on the circumstances.
analysis.excluded: People indirectly affected through cumulative impacts, market shifts, downstream effects, informal tenure not recognised in practice, or impacts beyond the project area may be excluded if the client's assessment does not capture them.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when livelihood restoration is time-limited, when company-managed processes are not connected to public land administration or social services, or when accountability weakens after financial close or project completion.
analysis.whyItMatters: Performance Standard 5 shows how displacement governance is increasingly mediated by private finance, ESG risk, lender requirements, and corporate due diligence. The political economy archetype is private-risk internalisation: displacement is governed to protect affected people and manage project risk, but public-system absorption remains external to the standard.

secondarySources.label: IFC Guidance Note 5 PDF

versions.institutionalForm: Private-sector environmental and social performance standard
versions.continuityEffect: IFC Performance Standard 5 extends displacement governance into private-sector finance, but responsibility remains anchored to client risk management and project boundaries rather than public-system inclusion.

cases.title: Thailand Migrant Health Insurance Scheme

policyRecord.whatItIs: Thailand's migrant health insurance arrangements provide a route for some non-Thai populations to access care through public health facilities outside the citizen universal coverage scheme. The scheme has been shaped by public health, migration management, labour administration, and financing concerns.
responsibleInstitutions: The Ministry of Public Health and linked health service administrators carry core system responsibility, while labour, interior, immigration, and employer-linked processes shape documentation and eligibility pathways.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility is tied to non-citizen registration, migrant status, health checks, premium payment, employer-linked documentation, or administrative windows rather than displacement need as such.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is bounded through premiums, facility reimbursement, health insurance fund arrangements, and public health budgets rather than an open-ended social entitlement.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Health records, migrant registration, work-permit records, health insurance fund records, and non-Thai identification systems intersect but are not always fully portable.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through ordinary public hospitals and clinics, making it more absorptive than parallel humanitarian health provision.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on whether registration and entitlement records follow the person across provinces, employers, facilities, and status categories.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is mainly administrative and facility-based, with limited rights-based appeal architecture for excluded non-citizens.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Ongoing sectoral arrangement with periodic administrative changes linked to migration policy, labour regularisation, and public health financing.

displacementTransition.trigger: movement across territorial boundary

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: public health financing and delivery system

mechanisms.scope: Coverage is strongest for migrants who can be documented, registered, linked to work-permit and health-check processes, or otherwise brought into the relevant administrative channel. Dependants may be included under specific arrangements.

Coverage is strongest for migrants who can be documented, registered, linked to work-permit and health-check processes, or otherwise brought into the relevant administrative channel. Dependants may be included under specific arrangements.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when a person's immigration status, employment status, province, facility, registration document, payment record, or entitlement record changes faster than health systems can update and transfer access.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when a person's immigration status, employment status, province, facility, registration document, payment record, or entitlement record changes faster than health systems can update and transfer access.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Non-citizens can enter health coverage through bounded administrative categories without broader legal membership.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The health ministry can act where public-health and hospital-financing interests align, while migration authorities retain status control.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Premium and cost-recovery design limits open-ended state exposure.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Non-citizens are absorbed into health financing where public health, hospital financing, and migration-management incentives align, without broader legal membership.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People without documents, people outside registration windows, refugees outside the relevant administrative channel, people unable to pay premiums, mobile workers, and people whose employer or status changes may remain outside or experience interrupted access.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Thailand's migrant health insurance architecture shows how a public system can absorb non-citizens for health purposes while leaving legal status, portability, and broader social protection unresolved.
analysis.governanceFunction: The scheme converts a migration and public health management problem into a bounded health-financing mechanism. It reduces uncompensated care, supports disease control, and allows access to public health facilities without generalising legal membership or broader social entitlement.
analysis.included: Coverage is strongest for migrants who can be documented, registered, linked to work-permit and health-check processes, or otherwise brought into the relevant administrative channel. Dependants may be included under specific arrangements.
analysis.excluded: People without documents, people outside registration windows, refugees outside the relevant administrative channel, people unable to pay premiums, mobile workers, and people whose employer or status changes may remain outside or experience interrupted access.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when a person's immigration status, employment status, province, facility, registration document, payment record, or entitlement record changes faster than health systems can update and transfer access.
analysis.whyItMatters: This is a key example of functional inclusion: the health system can include displaced or mobile people for sectoral reasons even when the legal system avoids broader recognition. The political economy archetype is sectoral absorption through public health logic: inclusion is feasible where state interests in disease control, hospital financing, and migration management align.

cases.title: Indonesia DTKS / SIKS-NG

populationGroups: Internally displaced people when captured by social welfare systems

policyRecord.whatItIs: DTKS is Indonesia's integrated social welfare data architecture, supported operationally by SIKS-NG and related administrative systems, for identifying and managing poor and vulnerable households across social assistance programmes.
responsibleInstitutions: The Ministry of Social Affairs holds national responsibility, while local governments play a major role in verification, validation, updating, and local-level implementation.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on registry status, poverty and vulnerability criteria, identity records, domicile information, local validation, and programme-specific criteria.
policyRecord.financing: The registry does not finance benefits itself, but it controls access to nationally and locally financed social assistance programmes.
policyRecord.dataSystems: DTKS and SIKS-NG are the central data layer, interacting with civil registration, local welfare lists, disaster records, programme-specific databases, and emerging socio-economic registration reforms.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery is programme-specific: cash transfers, food assistance, health subsidy links, education assistance, and other social assistance channels may use the registry.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is constrained when social assistance is tied to registered domicile, local verification, programme lists, or administrative cycles that do not update at displacement speed.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on grievance, correction, updating, and verification channels available through social affairs offices, village administrators, local governments, and programme mechanisms.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Ongoing administrative infrastructure with periodic updates, validation processes, and reform.

displacementTransition.trigger: movement across territorial boundary

institutionalInterfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Households whose poverty, vulnerability, identity, and domicile information are captured, validated, and updated in the registry can be routed toward assistance.
Households whose poverty, vulnerability, identity, and domicile information are captured, validated, and updated in the registry can be routed toward assistance.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks between disaster displacement lists and ordinary social welfare registries, and when a household moves across village, district, or province boundaries without data transfer, re-verification, or timely registry updating.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between disaster displacement lists and ordinary social welfare registries, and when a household moves across village, district, or province boundaries without data transfer, re-verification, or timely registry updating.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Households are included when poverty, identity and domicile are administratively verified.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Central social-welfare architecture depends on local updating and validation.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Registry inclusion controls programme exposure and can contract as readily as it expands.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Inclusion depends on whether households are visible, verified, updated, and classified within social welfare data systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Displaced households, informal settlers, people without current domicile documents, households newly impoverished by shocks, and households whose circumstances change between updating cycles may be missed or delayed.

analysis.oneLineFinding: DTKS and SIKS-NG can connect households to social assistance, but displaced households can fall out when emergency lists, domicile records, and registry updating cycles do not align.
analysis.governanceFunction: The system makes eligibility administratively legible. It determines who can be found, verified, updated, and linked to programmes across central and local government. In displacement contexts, its governance function is not only targeting but continuity: whether vulnerability remains visible after movement.
analysis.included: Households whose poverty, vulnerability, identity, and domicile information are captured, validated, and updated in the registry can be routed toward assistance.
analysis.excluded: Displaced households, informal settlers, people without current domicile documents, households newly impoverished by shocks, and households whose circumstances change between updating cycles may be missed or delayed.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between disaster displacement lists and ordinary social welfare registries, and when a household moves across village, district, or province boundaries without data transfer, re-verification, or timely registry updating.
analysis.whyItMatters: This entry maps the administrative infrastructure beneath social protection. Inclusion depends not only on policy intent but on whether data systems can follow people after displacement. The political economy archetype is registry-mediated inclusion: the state can include people whom it can identify, verify, and classify, while mobile or administratively irregular households risk becoming invisible.

cases.title: Philippines 4Ps

populationGroups: Internally displaced households when captured by programme systems

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Pantawid Pamilyang Pilipino Program is the Philippines' national conditional cash transfer programme, institutionalised by Republic Act No. 11310 and administered by the Department of Social Welfare and Development.
responsibleInstitutions: DSWD is the lead agency, with health, education, local government, payment providers, and community-level implementers carrying important implementation roles.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility is based on poverty targeting, household composition, programme registration, and compliance with health and education conditions.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is through national social protection appropriations and programme budgets, with possible links to shock response financing and emergency assistance during crises.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Programme records interact with household targeting systems, payment systems, school and health compliance monitoring, local case management, and grievance systems.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery operates through cash transfer mechanisms, municipal links, schools, health facilities, social welfare offices, and payment providers.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on whether beneficiary records, payment access, compliance monitoring, and case management can move across municipalities and displacement locations.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes programme grievance channels, DSWD oversight, audit, statutory responsibilities under the 4Ps law, and local implementation monitoring.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term statutory programme with recurring household support while eligibility and compliance continue.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

institutionalInterfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Poor households meeting programme criteria, particularly those with children or pregnant members, can be included when identified, registered, and maintained in programme systems.
Poor households meeting programme criteria, particularly those with children or pregnant members, can be included when identified, registered, and maintained in programme systems.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks between emergency displacement response and regular programme administration, especially when household records, school attendance, health visits, payment access, or municipal coordination do not travel with the family.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between emergency displacement response and regular programme administration, especially when household records, school attendance, health visits, payment access, or municipal coordination do not travel with the family.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Ordinary poverty eligibility can be extended administratively to disaster-affected households but displacement is not a permanent category.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: DSWD can expand an existing programme, but continuity depends on rules linking disaster response to regular enrolment.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: A statutory and budgeted programme can absorb temporary expansion, but longitudinal displacement costs remain bounded.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Displaced households can be stabilized through an ordinary social protection programme, but conditionality, registry status, and mobility can reproduce exclusion.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Displaced households may be missed if they are not in the targeting system, cannot maintain school or health compliance after moving, lack documentation, or cross local administrative boundaries without case transfer.

analysis.oneLineFinding: 4Ps gives the Philippines a statutory social protection platform, but displacement creates continuity risks when household location, school enrolment, health compliance, or registry status changes.
analysis.governanceFunction: 4Ps links poor households to cash assistance and human capital conditions, using social welfare administration as a platform for recurring support rather than one-off emergency relief. In displacement contexts, it can function as a stabilising platform if records, payments, and compliance systems remain portable.
analysis.included: Poor households meeting programme criteria, particularly those with children or pregnant members, can be included when identified, registered, and maintained in programme systems.
analysis.excluded: Displaced households may be missed if they are not in the targeting system, cannot maintain school or health compliance after moving, lack documentation, or cross local administrative boundaries without case transfer.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between emergency displacement response and regular programme administration, especially when household records, school attendance, health visits, payment access, or municipal coordination do not travel with the family.
analysis.whyItMatters: 4Ps is an absorption platform: it can stabilise households after shocks, but only if programme rules and data systems can accommodate mobility and protracted displacement. The political economy archetype is programme-based absorption with conditionality: displaced households can be included through ordinary social protection, but compliance and registry rules can reproduce exclusion.

relatedResearch: The Displacement Continuum and Sustainable Inclusion

cases.title: ASEAN Agreement on Disaster Management and Emergency Response

populationGroups: Communities at risk of disaster displacement

policyRecord.whatItIs: The ASEAN Agreement on Disaster Management and Emergency Response is the region's central legal framework for disaster cooperation. It establishes commitments on disaster risk reduction, preparedness, response, regional assistance, standby arrangements, and cooperation among ASEAN member states.
responsibleInstitutions: ASEAN member states carry primary responsibility. The AHA Centre, national disaster management offices, line ministries, and local authorities support coordination and implementation.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility is framed through disaster impact and national determination of disaster response needs rather than displacement status or long-term vulnerability.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on national disaster budgets, ASEAN cooperation mechanisms, external support, and humanitarian or development assistance. AADMER does not itself create household-level financing entitlements.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Disaster information systems, national disaster loss and damage data, AHA Centre coordination systems, needs assessments, and local records shape implementation.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through national disaster management agencies, ASEAN coordination, emergency response teams, local governments, humanitarian actors, and sectoral public systems.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited where assistance and registration are tied to place of disaster, evacuation site, or local authority jurisdiction.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is primarily intergovernmental and administrative. Individual complaints or enforceable claims depend on domestic law and national disaster systems.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Preparedness, emergency response, and recovery, though operational emphasis is often strongest during the emergency and early recovery phases.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: Populations affected by disasters within ASEAN member states, including people displaced or at risk of displacement by sudden-onset hazards, may benefit indirectly through national and regional disaster response mechanisms.

Populations affected by disasters within ASEAN member states, including people displaced or at risk of displacement by sudden-onset hazards, may benefit indirectly through national and regional disaster response mechanisms.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when regional emergency response ends before displaced households are connected to housing, land, livelihoods, social protection, civil registration, and local development planning.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when regional emergency response ends before displaced households are connected to housing, land, livelihoods, social protection, civil registration, and local development planning.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Disaster-affected populations are recognized through event-based cooperation rather than a durable displacement status.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Regional cooperation is strongest in preparedness and emergency response; recovery remains nationally implemented.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Regional cooperation does not establish durable fiscal responsibility for protracted displacement.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: ASEAN enables regional disaster cooperation, but assistance, recovery, and long-term inclusion depend on national and local systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People affected by conflict, persecution, development projects, slow-onset livelihood loss, or protracted post-disaster displacement may be weakly covered if their situation no longer fits emergency disaster response categories.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Regional cooperation with national implementation dependency

analysis.oneLineFinding: AADMER creates a binding regional disaster cooperation framework, but displacement continuity still depends on national systems, local authorities, and post-emergency recovery architecture.
analysis.governanceFunction: AADMER regionalizes disaster management without replacing national responsibility. Its governance function is to coordinate assistance, preparedness, and response across ASEAN, while leaving protection, registration, social assistance, land, housing, and recovery responsibilities largely within domestic systems.
analysis.included: Populations affected by disasters within ASEAN member states, including people displaced or at risk of displacement by sudden-onset hazards, may benefit indirectly through national and regional disaster response mechanisms.
analysis.excluded: People affected by conflict, persecution, development projects, slow-onset livelihood loss, or protracted post-disaster displacement may be weakly covered if their situation no longer fits emergency disaster response categories.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when regional emergency response ends before displaced households are connected to housing, land, livelihoods, social protection, civil registration, and local development planning.
analysis.whyItMatters: AADMER shows the strength and limits of regional disaster governance in Southeast Asia. It can mobilize cooperation for emergency response, but it does not itself solve the governance problem of whether disaster-displaced populations remain included after the emergency phase. The political economy archetype is regional emergency cooperation with national implementation dependency.

secondarySources.label: PreventionWeb AADMER Work Programme 2021-2025

relatedResearch: The Displacement Continuum and Sustainable Inclusion

versions.continuityEffect: AADMER creates a binding regional disaster cooperation framework, but displacement continuity still depends on national systems, local authorities, and post-emergency recovery architecture.

cases.title: ASEAN Five-Point Consensus on Myanmar

populationGroups: Refugees and cross-border populations indirectly affected

policyRecord.whatItIs: The ASEAN Five-Point Consensus was agreed in April 2021 as the regional political framework for addressing the Myanmar crisis. It calls for an immediate cessation of violence, constructive dialogue, appointment of a special envoy, humanitarian assistance through ASEAN mechanisms, and a special envoy visit to Myanmar.
responsibleInstitutions: ASEAN member states, the ASEAN Chair, the Special Envoy, the AHA Centre, Myanmar authorities, and humanitarian actors all hold partial roles, but no single mechanism carries comprehensive protection responsibility.
policyRecord.eligibility: No individual eligibility framework is created. Assistance depends on access, operational decisions, local conditions, and humanitarian arrangements.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on ASEAN assistance channels, donor contributions, humanitarian appeals, and bilateral support. The consensus does not create predictable displacement financing.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Data depends on humanitarian assessments, access-constrained reporting, UN and NGO monitoring, and ASEAN coordination channels. There is no integrated displacement registry.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery is mediated through ASEAN mechanisms, humanitarian partners, national or local authorities, and negotiated access arrangements.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is weak because assistance is tied to access routes, political permissions, and operational presence rather than portable entitlements.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is political and diplomatic. The consensus lacks direct enforcement, complaints, or rights-based accountability mechanisms for displaced people.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Crisis response framework, ongoing since 2021, with implementation dependent on political developments and access conditions.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: Conflict-affected civilians and displaced populations are indirectly included through the humanitarian assistance and dialogue dimensions, but not as rights-holders with enforceable claims.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks where humanitarian access is blocked, violence continues, ASEAN mechanisms cannot reach affected areas, or assistance is not connected to protection monitoring, local systems, or cross-border realities.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks where humanitarian access is blocked, violence continues, ASEAN mechanisms cannot reach affected areas, or assistance is not connected to protection monitoring, local systems, or cross-border realities.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Affected populations are addressed through humanitarian diplomacy without enforceable protection membership.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: ASEAN political consensus does not create operational authority sufficient to ensure access or continuity.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No durable financing or public-system absorption mechanism is created.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Regional diplomacy seeks humanitarian access and de-escalation while remaining constrained by sovereignty, consent, and limited enforcement.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People in contested areas, cross-border populations, refugees, undocumented displaced people, and communities outside accessible channels may remain outside assistance and protection mechanisms.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Five-Point Consensus provides ASEAN's political framework for Myanmar, but it has not created a reliable protection, access, or assistance architecture for displaced populations.
analysis.governanceFunction: The consensus creates a diplomatic and humanitarian access framework. It is not an IDP policy, protection instrument, refugee framework, or social assistance system. Its implementation depends on political consent, access negotiation, ASEAN unity, and the conduct of conflict parties.
analysis.included: Conflict-affected civilians and displaced populations are indirectly included through the humanitarian assistance and dialogue dimensions, but not as rights-holders with enforceable claims.
analysis.excluded: People in contested areas, cross-border populations, refugees, undocumented displaced people, and communities outside accessible channels may remain outside assistance and protection mechanisms.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks where humanitarian access is blocked, violence continues, ASEAN mechanisms cannot reach affected areas, or assistance is not connected to protection monitoring, local systems, or cross-border realities.
analysis.whyItMatters: The Five-Point Consensus shows the limits of regional diplomacy when displacement is produced by conflict and state violence. It is relevant to Mapping because it reveals a gap between regional political process and operational protection continuity. The political economy archetype is sovereignty-constrained humanitarian diplomacy.

cases.title: Thailand Universal Coverage Scheme

populationGroups: Poor and vulnerable households indirectly protected through universal coverage

policyRecord.whatItIs: Thailand's Universal Coverage Scheme is the tax-financed health coverage arrangement that expanded access to health care for Thai citizens not covered by other public insurance schemes. It is administered through the National Health Security Office and operates through ordinary public health facilities.
responsibleInstitutions: The National Health Security Office, Ministry of Public Health, public health facilities, and national budget authorities carry core responsibility.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility is based on Thai citizenship, registration, and scheme assignment within Thailand's health financing architecture.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is primarily tax-funded through national budget allocations, with capitation and provider payment arrangements administered through the health security system.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Civil registration, national ID, beneficiary registration, health facility records, and NHSO administrative systems shape access and continuity.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through ordinary public hospitals, clinics, primary care networks, referral systems, and health administrative mechanisms.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is stronger than in many sectoral schemes but still depends on registration, facility assignment, referral rules, and administrative updating.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes statutory responsibilities, administrative oversight, NHSO mechanisms, budget scrutiny, and health service complaints pathways.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term national health financing system.

displacementTransition.trigger: status reclassification

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: public health financing and delivery system

mechanisms.scope: Thai citizens eligible under the national health security system are included. The system is especially important for low-income citizens and populations outside civil servant or social security schemes.

Thai citizens eligible under the national health security system are included. The system is especially important for low-income citizens and populations outside civil servant or social security schemes.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks at the boundary between citizen-based entitlement and non-citizen or unresolved legal status. Access also depends on registration and administrative assignment within the health system.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks at the boundary between citizen-based entitlement and non-citizen or unresolved legal status. Access also depends on registration and administrative assignment within the health system.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Universal tax-financed entitlement is deep for citizens while non-citizens remain in separate schemes.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: A clear statutory purchaser and public delivery system sustain responsibility.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Tax financing converts health access into a recurrent national obligation for the covered population.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Health vulnerability is absorbed into a tax-financed universal system for citizens, while non-citizens remain governed through separate arrangements.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Non-citizens, undocumented migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, and some stateless or administratively unresolved populations are not automatically included through the citizen-based universal scheme, even if other arrangements may cover some groups.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Thailand's Universal Coverage Scheme demonstrates deep public-system absorption for citizens, while also revealing the categorical boundary separating citizen entitlement from non-citizen health inclusion.
analysis.governanceFunction: The scheme converts health access into a public financing and entitlement function. For displacement governance, it is important because it shows what full fiscal and institutional absorption looks like when a population is recognized as part of the national health membership system.
analysis.included: Thai citizens eligible under the national health security system are included. The system is especially important for low-income citizens and populations outside civil servant or social security schemes.
analysis.excluded: Non-citizens, undocumented migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, and some stateless or administratively unresolved populations are not automatically included through the citizen-based universal scheme, even if other arrangements may cover some groups.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks at the boundary between citizen-based entitlement and non-citizen or unresolved legal status. Access also depends on registration and administrative assignment within the health system.
analysis.whyItMatters: The Universal Coverage Scheme is a benchmark for sustainable inclusion because it shows how ordinary public systems can absorb vulnerability through national budgets, provider networks, and entitlement logic. The political economy archetype is universal citizen absorption with categorical exclusion at the border of membership.
