cases.title: Thailand Civil Registration and Statelessness Framework

populationGroups: Non-citizens with temporary or special documentation

policyRecord.whatItIs: Thailand has developed a complex set of civil registration, nationality, and legal status mechanisms for stateless and non-citizen populations, including populations in highland and border areas. Recent policy developments have accelerated pathways to legal status and nationality for large numbers of registered stateless people.
responsibleInstitutions: Interior authorities, civil registration offices, nationality authorities, local administrators, schools, health systems, and courts all shape recognition and access.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on documentation category, registration history, birth and parentage records, residence, cabinet resolutions, nationality law, and administrative verification.
policyRecord.financing: The framework does not itself finance benefits, but it determines access to publicly financed services and status pathways.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Civil registration, household registration, temporary ID systems, birth registration, nationality records, and local administrative databases are central.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through district offices, local administration, schools, health facilities, nationality processes, and related public service systems.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is variable. Documentation may not always carry equal access across provinces, schools, health facilities, or administrative categories.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on administrative review, documentation correction channels, nationality procedures, courts, human rights institutions, and civil society support.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term legal identity and membership architecture, with periodic reforms and accelerated status-resolution measures.

displacementTransition.trigger: movement across territorial boundary

mechanisms.scope: Registered stateless persons, people with recognised administrative records, children whose births are registered, and long-staying populations who meet legal or policy criteria may be included in pathways to legal status or nationality.

Registered stateless persons, people with recognised administrative records, children whose births are registered, and long-staying populations who meet legal or policy criteria may be included in pathways to legal status or nationality.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when identity documents are tied to a specific locality, when movement changes administrative jurisdiction, when birth registration does not translate into nationality, or when service systems require a stronger status than the person holds.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when identity documents are tied to a specific locality, when movement changes administrative jurisdiction, when birth registration does not translate into nationality, or when service systems require a stronger status than the person holds.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Administrative identity creates graduated membership without necessarily resolving nationality.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Interior and civil-registration authorities control the gateway to multiple public systems.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Recognition may unlock services but does not itself guarantee recurrent benefit financing.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Legal identity systems make people visible to the state and create staged pathways from documentation toward fuller membership and service access.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People without registration, people outside enumerated categories, mobile populations, unregistered children, and people whose records are incomplete or territorially tied may remain excluded or delayed.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Thailand's civil registration and statelessness framework can make non-citizens administratively visible, but service access and nationality pathways remain mediated by documentation category, residence history, and local administration.
analysis.governanceFunction: The framework makes people administratively legible. It can connect identity, residence, nationality claims, education, health, and movement permissions, but it also creates category-specific pathways where rights depend heavily on documentation status.
analysis.included: Registered stateless persons, people with recognised administrative records, children whose births are registered, and long-staying populations who meet legal or policy criteria may be included in pathways to legal status or nationality.
analysis.excluded: People without registration, people outside enumerated categories, mobile populations, unregistered children, and people whose records are incomplete or territorially tied may remain excluded or delayed.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when identity documents are tied to a specific locality, when movement changes administrative jurisdiction, when birth registration does not translate into nationality, or when service systems require a stronger status than the person holds.
analysis.whyItMatters: This framework is central to displacement governance because legal identity is the gateway to ordinary systems. It demonstrates that inclusion can begin administratively before full citizenship, but also that partial recognition can reproduce layered exclusion. The political economy archetype is registry-mediated membership formation.

cases.title: Indonesia Law No. 24/2007 on Disaster Management

policyRecord.whatItIs: Law No. 24 of 2007 is Indonesia's primary legal framework for disaster management. It establishes principles, institutional responsibilities, and arrangements for disaster risk reduction, emergency response, rehabilitation, and reconstruction, including the role of the national disaster management agency and regional agencies.
responsibleInstitutions: BNPB carries national coordination responsibility, while BPBDs, line ministries, local governments, and sectoral agencies carry implementation responsibilities.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on disaster status, location, impact assessment, evacuation or affected-person records, and local implementation rules.
policyRecord.financing: Financing comes through national and local disaster budgets, contingency funds, rehabilitation and reconstruction allocations, and external support where applicable.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Disaster impact assessments, evacuation records, BNPB/BPBD data, local population records, and sectoral beneficiary lists shape access and follow-up.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through BNPB, BPBDs, local governments, emergency services, shelters, public works, social affairs, health, education, and humanitarian partners.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited when assistance is tied to disaster location, local beneficiary records, or jurisdiction-specific recovery programmes.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on administrative oversight, disaster management procedures, local government responsibility, audit, grievance channels, and political monitoring.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Pre-disaster, emergency response, rehabilitation, and reconstruction, though displacement often extends beyond formal recovery cycles.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: People affected by officially recognised disasters, including those evacuated, displaced, injured, or otherwise affected by disaster impacts, may be included through disaster response and recovery mechanisms.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when emergency beneficiary lists do not transfer into social protection, civil registration, housing, livelihood support, or local planning systems after the immediate response period.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility is activated by disaster impact and emergency governance rather than displacement as a continuing condition.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: National emergency authority gives way to subnational recovery responsibility after the declared phase.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Extraordinary financing is time-bound; receiving districts absorb later costs through ordinary budgets.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: People become visible through disaster events and emergency administration, but continuity may weaken once the event-based response period ends.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People experiencing protracted displacement after the formal emergency phase, people moving outside the affected jurisdiction, informal settlers, and people affected by slow-onset or compound shocks may face gaps if they are no longer treated as disaster beneficiaries.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Indonesia's disaster management law creates a national-local disaster architecture, but displacement continuity depends on how emergency records connect to recovery, social protection, housing, and local administration.
analysis.governanceFunction: The law structures disaster management as a public responsibility across national and regional authorities. In displacement contexts, it defines the disaster governance channel through which affected populations may be identified, assisted, evacuated, and supported during recovery.
analysis.included: People affected by officially recognised disasters, including those evacuated, displaced, injured, or otherwise affected by disaster impacts, may be included through disaster response and recovery mechanisms.
analysis.excluded: People experiencing protracted displacement after the formal emergency phase, people moving outside the affected jurisdiction, informal settlers, and people affected by slow-onset or compound shocks may face gaps if they are no longer treated as disaster beneficiaries.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when emergency beneficiary lists do not transfer into social protection, civil registration, housing, livelihood support, or local planning systems after the immediate response period.
analysis.whyItMatters: This law is important because Indonesia has sophisticated disaster governance, yet displacement may still become administratively discontinuous after emergency response. The political economy archetype is event-based recognition with post-emergency discontinuity.

cases.title: Philippines Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act

policyRecord.whatItIs: Republic Act No. 10121 is the Philippines' Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act. It strengthens the national disaster risk reduction and management system, establishes the national framework, and organizes responsibilities across national agencies, local governments, and disaster risk reduction councils.
responsibleInstitutions: The National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council, Office of Civil Defense, line agencies, local DRRM councils, and local government units carry major responsibilities.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on disaster impact, local assessment, evacuation or affected-person status, and programme-specific criteria.
policyRecord.financing: Financing comes through national and local DRRM funds, local government budgets, recovery allocations, and external support where available.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Disaster assessments, evacuation records, local registries, damage and needs assessments, social welfare lists, and sectoral databases shape implementation.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through local governments, DRRM councils, emergency response systems, social welfare offices, schools, health facilities, and national agencies.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is constrained when assistance is tied to the locality of origin or evacuation site and records do not transfer easily across jurisdictions.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on statutory duties, local government oversight, audit, council structures, grievance mechanisms, and political accountability.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Prevention, preparedness, response, rehabilitation, and recovery, with displacement often continuing beyond formal response periods.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: Communities and households affected by disasters, including those evacuated or internally displaced by hazards, may be included through national and local DRRM mechanisms.
Communities and households affected by disasters, including those evacuated or internally displaced by hazards, may be included through national and local DRRM mechanisms.
Communities and households affected by disasters, including those evacuated or internally displaced by hazards, may be included through national and local DRRM mechanisms.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks between evacuation assistance and longer-term housing, livelihood, social protection, education, health, and local development planning.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between evacuation assistance and longer-term housing, livelihood, social protection, education, health, and local development planning.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Disaster-affected populations are recognized through event and jurisdiction-based mechanisms.

politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: National and local disaster funds improve predictability but remain event-linked.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Disaster governance is institutionalized nationally but depends heavily on local governments whose fiscal and administrative capacity determines continuity.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Displaced households may fall out when they move across local government jurisdictions, remain displaced beyond emergency cycles, lack documentation, or cannot access local recovery programmes.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Decentralized responsibility with local fiscal exposure

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Philippines DRRM Act institutionalises disaster governance across national and local levels, but displacement continuity depends on whether local systems can carry assistance beyond evacuation and emergency response.
analysis.governanceFunction: The Act decentralizes and institutionalizes disaster risk management. It connects preparedness, prevention, response, rehabilitation, and recovery to local government structures, making local capacity central to displacement outcomes.
analysis.included: Communities and households affected by disasters, including those evacuated or internally displaced by hazards, may be included through national and local DRRM mechanisms.
analysis.excluded: Displaced households may fall out when they move across local government jurisdictions, remain displaced beyond emergency cycles, lack documentation, or cannot access local recovery programmes.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between evacuation assistance and longer-term housing, livelihood, social protection, education, health, and local development planning.
analysis.whyItMatters: The Philippines has one of the region's most developed disaster governance frameworks, but disaster displacement still exposes local fiscal and administrative capacity constraints. The political economy archetype is decentralized disaster governance with local fiscal exposure.

cases.title: Philippines Marawi Rehabilitation Architecture

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Marawi rehabilitation architecture refers to the inter-agency recovery, reconstruction, and rehabilitation arrangements established after the 2017 Marawi siege, including Task Force Bangon Marawi and subsequent national and Bangsamoro-level recovery initiatives.
responsibleInstitutions: National agencies, Task Force Bangon Marawi, local government, BARMM institutions, housing agencies, social welfare bodies, public works agencies, and donors have all held roles.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on residence, displacement status, property claims, location in affected areas, household registration, and programme-specific criteria.
policyRecord.financing: Financing comes from national appropriations, rehabilitation pledges, donor support, housing and infrastructure budgets, and Bangsamoro or local recovery allocations.
policyRecord.dataSystems: IDP lists, household profiling, property records, damage assessments, shelter records, compensation lists, and local government data shape inclusion.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through inter-agency task force structures, local authorities, housing programmes, cash or livelihood assistance, infrastructure projects, and social services.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited when assistance is tied to place of origin, property status, temporary shelter location, or official household lists.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on inter-agency oversight, legislative scrutiny, local grievance channels, audit, beneficiary communication, and access to land and compensation processes.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Post-conflict recovery and reconstruction, with protracted displacement and return issues extending years beyond the emergency phase.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: Families displaced from Marawi, residents of most affected areas, home-based IDPs, households in temporary shelters, and affected communities may be included through assistance, housing, livelihood, and reconstruction programmes.

Families displaced from Marawi, residents of most affected areas, home-based IDPs, households in temporary shelters, and affected communities may be included through assistance, housing, livelihood, and reconstruction programmes.

Families displaced from Marawi, residents of most affected areas, home-based IDPs, households in temporary shelters, and affected communities may be included through assistance, housing, livelihood, and reconstruction programmes.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks between emergency aid, temporary shelter, compensation, land and property claims, permanent housing, livelihood recovery, and municipal service restoration.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between emergency aid, temporary shelter, compensation, land and property claims, permanent housing, livelihood recovery, and municipal service restoration.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Conflict-displaced households are recognized through a rehabilitation architecture but differentiated by location and eligibility.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Responsibility fragments across housing, education, livelihoods and local recovery institutions as displacement becomes protracted.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Reconstruction appropriations are substantial but programme- and phase-bounded.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Inclusion is pursued through reconstruction, compensation, housing, and local recovery, but land, documentation, and accountability bottlenecks shape outcomes.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Households without complete land or property documentation, renters, informal occupants, people outside official lists, and families whose displacement becomes protracted may face exclusion or delay.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Marawi recovery architecture shows how conflict displacement can shift from emergency response to reconstruction while many households remain caught between return, compensation, housing, and local recovery systems.
analysis.governanceFunction: The architecture coordinates reconstruction, housing, services, compensation, infrastructure, and assistance for displaced and affected populations. Its governance challenge is to convert a conflict-recovery programme into durable urban inclusion.
analysis.included: Families displaced from Marawi, residents of most affected areas, home-based IDPs, households in temporary shelters, and affected communities may be included through assistance, housing, livelihood, and reconstruction programmes.
analysis.excluded: Households without complete land or property documentation, renters, informal occupants, people outside official lists, and families whose displacement becomes protracted may face exclusion or delay.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between emergency aid, temporary shelter, compensation, land and property claims, permanent housing, livelihood recovery, and municipal service restoration.
analysis.whyItMatters: Marawi is a critical Southeast Asian case of conflict-related urban displacement. It shows that durable solutions depend not only on ending hostilities but on land, housing, compensation, infrastructure, local governance, and trust. The political economy archetype is reconstruction-led inclusion with land and accountability bottlenecks.

cases.title: Malaysia Foreign Worker Health Protection Scheme

policyRecord.whatItIs: Malaysia has used employment-linked insurance and health financing arrangements for foreign workers, alongside public hospital charging rules and migrant worker administration. These mechanisms sit at the intersection of labour migration, health financing, and immigration control.
responsibleInstitutions: Health authorities, labour authorities, immigration agencies, employers, insurers, and public hospitals all shape access.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on documented foreign worker status, employment, valid insurance or coverage, employer compliance, and facility rules.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is employment-linked and insurance-mediated, supplemented by out-of-pocket payments and public hospital charging systems.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Work permit records, immigration data, employer records, insurance enrolment, and hospital administrative records shape inclusion.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery occurs through public and private health facilities, employer or insurer-mediated arrangements, and migrant worker administrative channels.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is weak where coverage is tied to employer, permit validity, insurance status, or legal stay.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on labour enforcement, health facility procedures, insurance rules, employer obligations, and migrant complaint channels.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Ongoing employment-linked arrangement subject to migration policy and health financing changes.

displacementTransition.trigger: status reclassification

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: public health financing and delivery system

mechanisms.scope: Documented foreign workers whose employment, insurance, and administrative status bring them within the relevant health protection or payment arrangements.
Documented foreign workers whose employment, insurance, and administrative status bring them within the relevant health protection or payment arrangements.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when workers change employers, lose documented status, become irregular, fall outside insurance validity, or require care beyond covered benefits.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when workers change employers, lose documented status, become irregular, fall outside insurance validity, or require care beyond covered benefits.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Health access is tied to lawful employment and administrative registration rather than vulnerability.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Labour and health institutions cooperate while immigration status remains controlling.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Employer- or worker-linked financing bounds public exposure.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Health access and cost management are tied to documented employment and insurance, leaving people outside labour channels structurally exposed.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Undocumented migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, dependants outside employment-linked arrangements, people who lose work status, and people unable to pay charges may be excluded or face high barriers.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Malaysia's foreign worker health protection architecture links some migrant health access to employment and insurance, but refugees, undocumented migrants, and people outside labour channels remain structurally exposed.
analysis.governanceFunction: The scheme manages health costs and access for documented foreign workers without creating general non-citizen health inclusion. Its function is to internalize some health risk into labour migration administration and employer-linked arrangements.
analysis.included: Documented foreign workers whose employment, insurance, and administrative status bring them within the relevant health protection or payment arrangements.
analysis.excluded: Undocumented migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, dependants outside employment-linked arrangements, people who lose work status, and people unable to pay charges may be excluded or face high barriers.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when workers change employers, lose documented status, become irregular, fall outside insurance validity, or require care beyond covered benefits.
analysis.whyItMatters: Malaysia illustrates a different form of sectoral inclusion from Thailand: health access is more closely tied to employment and migration control than to universal public health logic. The political economy archetype is employment-linked containment of health costs.

cases.title: Myanmar Maternal and Child Cash Transfer

policyRecord.whatItIs: Myanmar's Maternal and Child Cash Transfer programme was developed as a nutrition-sensitive social protection intervention focused on the first 1,000 days of life. It provided cash support to pregnant women and mothers of young children, with implementation and financing support evolving over time.
responsibleInstitutions: Myanmar's social welfare authorities, health and nutrition actors, local administrators, implementing partners, and development financiers have all played roles.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on pregnancy or child age, programme geography, household registration, enrolment processes, and implementation rules.
policyRecord.financing: Financing has depended on government commitments, development partner support, project financing, and programme-specific resources.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Beneficiary registries, maternal and child health records, payment records, social welfare databases, and local administrative lists shape inclusion.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery may include cash payments, social and behaviour change communication, local social welfare administration, health outreach, and partner-supported implementation.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is constrained when benefits are linked to programme geography, local verification, payment access, or administrative continuity.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on programme grievance systems, social welfare administration, donor oversight, monitoring, and local implementation channels.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Early-life social protection programme with potential long-term welfare effects, but vulnerable to political and economic disruption.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

institutionalInterfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Pregnant women, mothers, and young children in programme areas who meet eligibility and registration conditions may be included.
Pregnant women, mothers, and young children in programme areas who meet eligibility and registration conditions may be included.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when payment systems, social welfare offices, beneficiary records, nutrition messaging, and local access are disrupted by conflict, mobility, economic crisis, or institutional breakdown.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when payment systems, social welfare offices, beneficiary records, nutrition messaging, and local access are disrupted by conflict, mobility, economic crisis, or institutional breakdown.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Inclusion is framed through maternal and child vulnerability rather than displacement status.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Social-welfare and health mandates permit bounded inclusion but depend on functioning state delivery.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Programme financing can become recurrent, but crisis and territorial fragmentation undermine continuity.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Early social protection infrastructure can reduce vulnerability, but crisis, conflict, and administrative disruption expose the fragility of programme continuity.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Households outside programme areas, displaced households not captured by registration, families moving across townships, and people in inaccessible or conflict-affected areas may be excluded.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Nascent social protection under crisis stress

analysis.oneLineFinding: Myanmar's MCCT shows how early social protection systems can create resilience before crisis, but conflict, economic collapse, and administrative disruption expose the fragility of programme continuity.
analysis.governanceFunction: The programme links social protection, nutrition, human capital, and household resilience. In displacement governance terms, it shows how national social assistance can reduce vulnerability before shocks, but also how programme continuity depends on political and administrative stability.
analysis.included: Pregnant women, mothers, and young children in programme areas who meet eligibility and registration conditions may be included.
analysis.excluded: Households outside programme areas, displaced households not captured by registration, families moving across townships, and people in inaccessible or conflict-affected areas may be excluded.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when payment systems, social welfare offices, beneficiary records, nutrition messaging, and local access are disrupted by conflict, mobility, economic crisis, or institutional breakdown.
analysis.whyItMatters: The MCCT demonstrates that social protection can generate durable welfare benefits, but it also shows why displacement-sensitive systems need portability, crisis financing, and protected administrative capacity. The political economy archetype is nascent social protection under crisis stress.

cases.title: Bangladesh-UNHCR Rohingya Registration and Identity Architecture

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Government of Bangladesh and UNHCR have implemented joint verification and registration processes for Rohingya refugees, including biometric identity management and issuance of identity documents for assistance, protection, and population management in Cox's Bazar.
responsibleInstitutions: The Government of Bangladesh, UNHCR, humanitarian agencies, camp authorities, and protection actors share operational roles, while durable solutions depend on broader political and regional processes.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on registration, verification, household composition, identity records, arrival status, and operational rules for assistance and protection services.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on humanitarian appeals, donor support, UNHCR and partner budgets, and host-state contributions to camp administration and infrastructure.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Biometric registration, household data, identity documents, protection records, assistance databases, and camp management systems form the core data architecture.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery operates through camp-based humanitarian services, food assistance, health, education, shelter, protection casework, and partner referral systems.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited. Identity documents support access within the humanitarian response but do not confer national legal status or easy cross-border recognition.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on data protection safeguards, complaints mechanisms, humanitarian accountability systems, government oversight, and UNHCR protection standards.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Humanitarian registration architecture for a protracted refugee situation, with uncertain transition to durable solutions.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: Registered Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh, particularly those verified through joint processes and issued identity documents, are included in assistance and protection systems.
Registered Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh, particularly those verified through joint processes and issued identity documents, are included in assistance and protection systems.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks because registration supports humanitarian access but not durable legal residence, freedom of movement, labour market inclusion, national social protection, or secure return conditions.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks because registration supports humanitarian access but not durable legal residence, freedom of movement, labour market inclusion, national social protection, or secure return conditions.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Registration creates humanitarian legibility without full domestic legal membership.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Government and UNHCR share registration functions while service delivery remains largely humanitarian.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Financing is principally humanitarian and externally conditioned.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugees are made visible for assistance and protection while remaining outside durable legal membership and ordinary national systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Unregistered new arrivals, people who avoid registration, people with contested data, people outside camp systems, and those whose status or identity records are disputed may face exclusion or restricted access.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Bangladesh-UNHCR registration architecture gives Rohingya refugees operational identity for assistance and protection, but it does not create durable legal status, national inclusion, or portable membership.
analysis.governanceFunction: The architecture makes the refugee population visible for humanitarian assistance, family linkage, protection casework, and service delivery. It also supports host-state population management without transforming refugees into members of national systems.
analysis.included: Registered Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh, particularly those verified through joint processes and issued identity documents, are included in assistance and protection systems.
analysis.excluded: Unregistered new arrivals, people who avoid registration, people with contested data, people outside camp systems, and those whose status or identity records are disputed may face exclusion or restricted access.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks because registration supports humanitarian access but not durable legal residence, freedom of movement, labour market inclusion, national social protection, or secure return conditions.
analysis.whyItMatters: This is a central case for displacement governance because data inclusion does not equal system inclusion. Refugees may be highly visible to humanitarian systems while remaining outside national membership and durable solutions. The political economy archetype is humanitarian legibility without legal absorption.

cases.title: ASEAN Declaration on the Protection and Promotion of the Rights of Migrant Workers

policyRecord.whatItIs: The ASEAN Declaration on the Protection and Promotion of the Rights of Migrant Workers was adopted in 2007 as a regional commitment to promote fair treatment, welfare, dignity, and protection for migrant workers. It recognizes responsibilities of both sending and receiving states while preserving national implementation discretion.
responsibleInstitutions: Sending and receiving states share responsibilities, with labour ministries, immigration authorities, recruitment regulators, employers, consulates, ASEAN bodies, and civil society all playing roles.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on migrant-worker status, employment relationship, documentation, national labour law, immigration status, and bilateral arrangements.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on employer obligations, worker contributions, social security arrangements, recruitment regulation, national welfare funds, and consular support.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Work permits, recruitment records, immigration data, employer records, social security databases, consular records, and labour inspection systems shape inclusion.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through labour departments, immigration systems, employers, recruitment agencies, consulates, courts, social security systems, shelters, and grievance mechanisms.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited where rights and benefits are tied to employer, permit, contract, country of work, or documented status.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on national labour enforcement, complaint channels, consular protection, regional follow-up, employer regulation, and access to remedies.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term regional labour migration framework with continuing relevance to ASEAN mobility governance.

displacementTransition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: Migrant workers moving within or from ASEAN countries may be included where national systems and bilateral arrangements recognize their labour, immigration, and welfare status.
Migrant workers moving within or from ASEAN countries may be included where national systems and bilateral arrangements recognize their labour, immigration, and welfare status.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when recruitment, employment, residence, health insurance, social security, and complaints systems are not portable across employers, sectors, borders, or status changes.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when recruitment, employment, residence, health insurance, social security, and complaints systems are not portable across employers, sectors, borders, or status changes.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Protection is strongest for regular migrant workers and weakest for irregular or displaced populations.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Regional commitments depend on national labour and migration implementation.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No regional financing obligation is created.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: ASEAN recognises migrant-worker protection while leaving enforcement, eligibility, and portability to national labour and immigration systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Undocumented workers, dependants, refugees working informally, trafficking survivors outside labour channels, and workers whose employment status changes may fall outside effective protection.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Managed labour mobility with bounded protection

analysis.oneLineFinding: The ASEAN migrant workers declaration recognizes migrant-worker protection as a regional concern, but access to rights remains mediated by national labour, immigration, recruitment, and social protection systems.
analysis.governanceFunction: The declaration provides a regional cooperation framework for labour migration without creating directly enforceable individual entitlements. It links recruitment regulation, labour protection, documentation, welfare, and state cooperation, but leaves implementation to domestic law and bilateral or regional arrangements.
analysis.included: Migrant workers moving within or from ASEAN countries may be included where national systems and bilateral arrangements recognize their labour, immigration, and welfare status.
analysis.excluded: Undocumented workers, dependants, refugees working informally, trafficking survivors outside labour channels, and workers whose employment status changes may fall outside effective protection.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when recruitment, employment, residence, health insurance, social security, and complaints systems are not portable across employers, sectors, borders, or status changes.
analysis.whyItMatters: The declaration is important for displacement governance because many displaced or crisis-affected people move through labour migration channels rather than refugee or IDP systems. The political economy archetype is managed labour mobility with bounded protection: states recognise worker welfare while maintaining control over admission, status, and enforcement.

cases.title: Indonesia Presidential Regulation No. 125/2016 on the Handling of Refugees from Abroad

policyRecord.whatItIs: Presidential Regulation No. 125/2016 on the Handling of Refugees from Abroad provides Indonesia's principal administrative framework for dealing with foreign refugees. It addresses rescue, discovery, shelter, coordination, security, and cooperation with international organizations.
responsibleInstitutions: The regulation assigns roles to central government, local government, immigration, police, rescue authorities, and relevant ministries, with UNHCR and international organizations playing major operational roles.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on being identified as a refugee or asylum seeker from abroad and falling within the administrative handling mechanisms established by the regulation.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on national and local administrative resources, international organizations, humanitarian funding, and partner support. The regulation does not create a comprehensive public financing entitlement.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Refugee identification, immigration records, UNHCR registration, shelter records, and coordination data shape implementation.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through reception, shelters, local authorities, immigration offices, rescue agencies, UNHCR, IOM, and humanitarian partners.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited because refugees do not receive durable national status and may depend on location-specific shelter or assistance arrangements.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is administrative and coordination-based, with limited direct rights-based enforcement for refugees under domestic law.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Administrative framework for ongoing refugee reception and management, often applied in protracted situations.

displacementTransition.trigger: status reclassification

mechanisms.scope: Foreign refugees and asylum seekers found or rescued in Indonesia may be included in reception, shelter, coordination, and referral arrangements.
Foreign refugees and asylum seekers found or rescued in Indonesia may be included in reception, shelter, coordination, and referral arrangements.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks between reception and long-term legal status, between shelter and ordinary housing, between humanitarian assistance and public services, and between temporary presence and durable solutions.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between reception and long-term legal status, between shelter and ordinary housing, between humanitarian assistance and public services, and between temporary presence and durable solutions.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Refugees are recognized as a distinct administrative caseload without general legal or socioeconomic inclusion.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The regulation assigns cross-sector roles but remains focused on handling rather than longitudinal public-system responsibility.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Implementation relies heavily on humanitarian and local arrangements rather than recurrent inclusion budgets.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugees are administratively received, sheltered, and coordinated without being absorbed into a full asylum, work, or national inclusion system.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People outside official handling channels, undocumented migrants not recognized as asylum seekers, long-staying refugees needing work or education access, and people seeking durable local integration remain weakly covered.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Indonesia's refugee regulation creates an administrative framework for handling refugees from abroad, but it does not establish a full asylum system, durable status, or ordinary service inclusion.
analysis.governanceFunction: The regulation organizes refugee handling as an administrative and coordination matter rather than a full status-determination or integration system. It clarifies roles for national and local authorities while leaving refugee protection heavily dependent on cooperation with UNHCR and IOM.
analysis.included: Foreign refugees and asylum seekers found or rescued in Indonesia may be included in reception, shelter, coordination, and referral arrangements.
analysis.excluded: People outside official handling channels, undocumented migrants not recognized as asylum seekers, long-staying refugees needing work or education access, and people seeking durable local integration remain weakly covered.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between reception and long-term legal status, between shelter and ordinary housing, between humanitarian assistance and public services, and between temporary presence and durable solutions.
analysis.whyItMatters: The regulation is one of Southeast Asia's clearest examples of administrative refugee governance without accession to the 1951 Refugee Convention. The political economy archetype is humanitarian containment through administrative coordination: refugees are managed and assisted, but not absorbed into national membership or welfare systems.

cases.title: Thailand Refugee Camp and Border Assistance Architecture

policyRecord.whatItIs: Thailand has hosted refugees from Myanmar for decades in temporary shelters along the border. The architecture combines Thai administrative control, humanitarian assistance, UNHCR protection engagement, NGO service delivery, and restrictions on movement and formal integration.
responsibleInstitutions: Thai authorities, camp administration, UNHCR, NGOs, community-based organizations, donors, and refugee committees all carry roles within a constrained protection environment.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on camp recognition, registration status, residence in designated shelters, protection assessment, and operational rules.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends largely on humanitarian donors, NGO delivery, UNHCR support, and host-state administrative contributions rather than full national budget absorption.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Camp records, registration databases, UNHCR data, NGO beneficiary lists, education and health records, and assistance systems shape inclusion.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery occurs through camps, humanitarian agencies, community structures, health and education NGOs, protection partners, and Thai administrative oversight.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is weak because assistance, documentation, and services are largely tied to camp residence and recognized status.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on humanitarian accountability systems, Thai administrative oversight, UNHCR protection engagement, donor requirements, and community mechanisms.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Protracted humanitarian assistance architecture with periodic repatriation, resettlement, and self-reliance discussions.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: Registered or camp-recognized refugees and residents of designated temporary shelters may receive humanitarian assistance, protection support, education, health services, and camp-based services.
Registered or camp-recognized refugees and residents of designated temporary shelters may receive humanitarian assistance, protection support, education, health services, and camp-based services.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when people move outside camps, when humanitarian assistance is not connected to national health, education, labour, social protection, or civil registration systems, and when durable solutions remain politically constrained.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when people move outside camps, when humanitarian assistance is not connected to national health, education, labour, social protection, or civil registration systems, and when durable solutions remain politically constrained.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Camp populations are administratively tolerated and assisted through a territorially bounded humanitarian category.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Humanitarian actors carry service responsibility without a durable transfer into national systems.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: External humanitarian finance substitutes for recurrent state expenditure.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugees receive protection and assistance in bounded camp spaces while movement, work, legal status, and national-system access remain constrained.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Urban asylum seekers, unregistered arrivals, people outside camps, refugees who move for work, and people whose status is not recognized may have weaker access to assistance and protection.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Thailand's border camp architecture has provided long-term refuge and assistance for Myanmar refugees, but protection remains territorially bounded and weakly connected to ordinary national systems.
analysis.governanceFunction: The system manages refugee protection through humanitarian containment rather than national-system absorption. It provides safety and assistance in designated areas while preserving the state's discretion over status, movement, work, and long-term integration.
analysis.included: Registered or camp-recognized refugees and residents of designated temporary shelters may receive humanitarian assistance, protection support, education, health services, and camp-based services.
analysis.excluded: Urban asylum seekers, unregistered arrivals, people outside camps, refugees who move for work, and people whose status is not recognized may have weaker access to assistance and protection.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when people move outside camps, when humanitarian assistance is not connected to national health, education, labour, social protection, or civil registration systems, and when durable solutions remain politically constrained.
analysis.whyItMatters: This is a defining Southeast Asian case of protracted refugee governance. The political economy archetype is territorial humanitarian containment: refugees are protected and assisted in bounded spaces while broader legal and fiscal inclusion is avoided.

cases.title: Philippines Migrant Workers and Overseas Filipinos Act

policyRecord.whatItIs: Republic Act No. 8042, as amended by Republic Act No. 10022, establishes the Philippines' legal framework for protecting migrant workers and overseas Filipinos. It covers overseas employment policy, recruitment regulation, welfare assistance, legal support, repatriation, and state responsibility toward citizens abroad.
responsibleInstitutions: The Philippine state, labour migration agencies, consulates, welfare agencies, recruitment regulators, employers, destination-country authorities, and legal assistance systems share roles.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on Filipino nationality, overseas worker or migrant status, documentation, employment situation, welfare registration, and programme-specific criteria.
policyRecord.financing: Financing includes state budget allocations, migrant welfare funds, employer and recruitment-related obligations, legal assistance funds, and repatriation resources.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Deployment records, recruitment records, welfare membership data, consular records, case management systems, and destination-country employment information shape protection.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through labour migration agencies, consulates, welfare offices, legal assistance, repatriation systems, recruitment regulation, and reintegration programmes.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is comparatively strong for citizens but depends on documentation, consular access, destination-country cooperation, and continuity after return.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes courts, administrative enforcement, recruitment sanctions, consular casework, legislative oversight, audit, and migrant welfare mechanisms.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Full migration cycle: recruitment, deployment, employment abroad, distress response, repatriation, and reintegration.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

institutionalInterfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Overseas Filipino workers, migrant workers, and overseas Filipinos in distress may be covered through welfare, legal, labour, consular, and repatriation mechanisms.
Overseas Filipino workers, migrant workers, and overseas Filipinos in distress may be covered through welfare, legal, labour, consular, and repatriation mechanisms.
Overseas Filipino workers, migrant workers, and overseas Filipinos in distress may be covered through welfare, legal, labour, consular, and repatriation mechanisms.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks between recruitment and employment abroad, between Philippine protection systems and destination-country labour enforcement, and between repatriation and reintegration into domestic social protection and livelihoods systems.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between recruitment and employment abroad, between Philippine protection systems and destination-country labour enforcement, and between repatriation and reintegration into domestic social protection and livelihoods systems.

politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Dedicated institutions maintain responsibility beyond national territory.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Contributory and public welfare mechanisms bound support to a recognized worker category.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The state extends protection to nationals abroad through labour migration, consular, welfare, repatriation, and reintegration systems.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Workers outside documented channels, irregular migrants, people whose status changes abroad, dependants in complex situations, and migrants in inaccessible locations may face weaker practical protection.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Philippines migrant workers law builds a transnational protection architecture for overseas workers, but its portability depends on recruitment regulation, consular reach, employer compliance, and destination-country systems.
analysis.governanceFunction: The law extends state protection beyond national territory through labour migration governance. It links recruitment regulation, deployment, welfare funds, legal assistance, consular protection, and repatriation into a transnational administrative system.
analysis.included: Overseas Filipino workers, migrant workers, and overseas Filipinos in distress may be covered through welfare, legal, labour, consular, and repatriation mechanisms.
analysis.excluded: Workers outside documented channels, irregular migrants, people whose status changes abroad, dependants in complex situations, and migrants in inaccessible locations may face weaker practical protection.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between recruitment and employment abroad, between Philippine protection systems and destination-country labour enforcement, and between repatriation and reintegration into domestic social protection and livelihoods systems.
analysis.whyItMatters: The law is important for displacement governance because it shows a strong model of mobility-related state responsibility beyond territory. The political economy archetype is citizenship-based transnational protection: the state protects nationals abroad more robustly than many systems protect non-citizens within their territory.

cases.title: Vietnam Residence Law and Household Registration Reform

policyRecord.whatItIs: Vietnam's 2020 Residence Law and related reforms shifted residence management away from paper household registration books toward electronic residence data and national population databases. The reform changes how citizens prove residence and identity for administrative purposes.
responsibleInstitutions: Public security authorities, local administrations, population database managers, service agencies, and administrative procedure offices carry implementation responsibilities.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on citizenship, lawful residence, registration rules, household or owner consent in some cases, and digital record accuracy.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is part of broader state administrative modernization and population database investment rather than a displacement-specific funding stream.
policyRecord.dataSystems: National population databases, citizen ID systems, residence records, digital administrative platforms, and local registration systems are central.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through public security offices, local administrations, online residence registration systems, and service agencies using residence data.
policyRecord.portability: Portability improves where digital records replace paper books, but practical portability depends on data accuracy, interagency use, and local acceptance.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on administrative correction procedures, appeals, local administrative oversight, and access to updated digital records.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term administrative modernization with continuing implications for internal mobility and service access.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: Vietnamese citizens with registered residence and updated digital records may benefit from simplified administrative procedures and reduced dependence on paper household books.
Vietnamese citizens with registered residence and updated digital records may benefit from simplified administrative procedures and reduced dependence on paper household books.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when actual residence, digital residence data, local registration, and service eligibility do not align, especially for mobile, poor, or informally housed populations.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when actual residence, digital residence data, local registration, and service eligibility do not align, especially for mobile, poor, or informally housed populations.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Citizen access is mediated through residence registration and digital administrative identity.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Public-security registration architecture remains the gateway to locally delivered services.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Local service budgets remain territorially allocated even as registration becomes more portable.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Digital residence data can reduce paper-based exclusion, but access still depends on local registration, data accuracy, and administrative acceptance.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People without stable lawful residence, informal migrants, renters unable to meet local registration requirements, and people whose digital records are incomplete or outdated may continue to face barriers.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Vietnam's residence reform reduces reliance on paper household registration, but access to services still depends on how digital residence data translate into portable administrative inclusion.
analysis.governanceFunction: The reform modernizes the administrative infrastructure that connects people to place, services, and legal transactions. In displacement terms, it is relevant because residence registration can either enable or block access when people move internally.
analysis.included: Vietnamese citizens with registered residence and updated digital records may benefit from simplified administrative procedures and reduced dependence on paper household books.
analysis.excluded: People without stable lawful residence, informal migrants, renters unable to meet local registration requirements, and people whose digital records are incomplete or outdated may continue to face barriers.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when actual residence, digital residence data, local registration, and service eligibility do not align, especially for mobile, poor, or informally housed populations.
analysis.whyItMatters: Residence registration is a core governance layer in the displacement continuum. Reform can improve portability, but digitalization does not automatically solve exclusion if service access remains tied to local registration. The political economy archetype is digital administrative portability with residual local gatekeeping.

cases.title: Cambodia Land Law and Economic Land Concession Architecture

policyRecord.whatItIs: Cambodia's 2001 Land Law establishes the legal framework for land ownership, possession, state land, concessions, and related land administration. It is central to understanding development-induced displacement, land conflict, economic concessions, and tenure security.
responsibleInstitutions: Land ministries, cadastral authorities, local governments, concession-granting authorities, courts, investors, and dispute resolution bodies shape implementation.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on ownership, possession, tenure recognition, land classification, concession boundaries, indigenous community recognition, and compensation rules.
policyRecord.financing: Financing for compensation or resettlement depends on project budgets, investor obligations, state programmes, donor-supported projects, and dispute resolution outcomes.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Cadastral records, land titles, concession maps, state land classifications, community land records, dispute files, and project impact assessments shape inclusion.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through land administration, cadastral processes, concession governance, courts, local authorities, resettlement arrangements, and project implementation structures.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited where livelihoods, community identity, and land-based claims are tied to specific territory and cannot be transferred through compensation alone.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on courts, cadastral dispute resolution, administrative oversight, concession compliance, human rights mechanisms, and community advocacy.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term land governance framework with continuing relevance to development, investment, and resettlement.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People with recognized ownership, lawful possession, or eligible claims may be included within land protection and compensation frameworks. Indigenous communities may have collective land recognition pathways under relevant provisions and procedures.

People with recognized ownership, lawful possession, or eligible claims may be included within land protection and compensation frameworks. Indigenous communities may have collective land recognition pathways under relevant provisions and procedures.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when land recognition does not match actual occupation, when compensation is tied to formal title, when concession decisions override local use, or when resettlement is disconnected from livelihoods and services.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when land recognition does not match actual occupation, when compensation is tied to formal title, when concession decisions override local use, or when resettlement is disconnected from livelihoods and services.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Formal title and legally recognized use determine whether affected people enter compensation and remedy systems.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Land and concession institutions prioritize investment administration, with fragmented post-displacement responsibility.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Compensation is transaction- or project-bounded rather than integrated into continuing public support.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Formal land law protects recognized claims while communities with weak, informal, or contested tenure remain vulnerable to investment-driven displacement.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Informal settlers, people without formal documents, communities on contested state land, renters, mobile groups, and households affected by concession-related pressures may be weakly protected.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Cambodia's land law provides a legal architecture for ownership and concessions, but displacement risk emerges where tenure recognition, concession governance, and compensation systems fail to protect affected communities.
analysis.governanceFunction: The law structures the relationship between land, state authority, private investment, community tenure, and compensation. In displacement terms, it determines who is recognized as having protectable land rights when development, concessions, or state land allocation affect communities.
analysis.included: People with recognized ownership, lawful possession, or eligible claims may be included within land protection and compensation frameworks. Indigenous communities may have collective land recognition pathways under relevant provisions and procedures.
analysis.excluded: Informal settlers, people without formal documents, communities on contested state land, renters, mobile groups, and households affected by concession-related pressures may be weakly protected.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when land recognition does not match actual occupation, when compensation is tied to formal title, when concession decisions override local use, or when resettlement is disconnected from livelihoods and services.
analysis.whyItMatters: Cambodia is a key case for development-induced displacement in Southeast Asia. The political economy archetype is land formalization under investment pressure: formal law can protect some claims while making unregistered or politically weak communities vulnerable.

responsibleInstitutions: Project developers, responsible ministries, local authorities, environmental and social agencies, and project implementation bodies share roles in compensation, resettlement, and monitoring.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on being recognized as affected by a development project, documented losses, project impact assessment, cut-off dates, and resettlement planning processes.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is primarily project-based, through developer or project budgets, with possible support from public agencies or development financiers.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Impact assessments, land and asset inventories, socio-economic surveys, resettlement plans, compensation records, and monitoring reports shape inclusion.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through project developers, local authorities, resettlement committees, compensation mechanisms, livelihood restoration programmes, and monitoring systems.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited because entitlements are tied to the project, affected area, and relocation site rather than portable social protection or land systems.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on grievance procedures, administrative oversight, project monitoring, lender safeguards where applicable, and affected community participation.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Project cycle and post-resettlement monitoring, though livelihood and community impacts may continue much longer.

analysis.excluded: People with informal or undocumented land use, indirectly affected households, downstream communities, seasonal users, and people whose livelihood losses persist after relocation may be weakly covered.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when compensation is paid but livelihoods are not restored, when relocated communities lose access to land or services, or when monitoring ends before social and economic recovery is achieved.
analysis.whyItMatters: Lao PDR is a central case for hydropower, infrastructure, and development-induced displacement. The political economy archetype is project-led resettlement with livelihood restoration risk.

cases.title: Thailand Cabinet Resolution on Accelerated Statelessness Resolution

policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through district offices, nationality procedures, local verification, civil registration systems, and related public service agencies.

cases.title: UNHCR Registration as Humanitarian Identity Infrastructure

policyRecord.whatItIs: UNHCR registration and identity management systems record and document refugees, asylum seekers, stateless persons, and other people of concern for protection, assistance, case management, and solutions purposes. In many countries, UNHCR registration operates alongside or in place of state asylum systems.
responsibleInstitutions: UNHCR, host governments, registration partners, data protection officers, humanitarian agencies, and service providers all hold roles depending on the operational context.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on UNHCR mandate, protection claim, national arrangements, operational access, and registration procedures.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on UNHCR budgets, donor contributions, humanitarian operations, and partner implementation resources.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Registration databases, biometric systems where used, case management tools, assistance databases, referral systems, identity documents, and protection records are central.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through registration centres, mobile registration, community outreach, protection casework, assistance systems, partner referrals, and documentation processes.
policyRecord.portability: Portability varies widely. UNHCR documentation may support access in some settings but may not be accepted by public systems or across borders.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on data protection standards, complaints mechanisms, UNHCR protection procedures, partner accountability, and host-state recognition.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Immediate protection and assistance through protracted displacement and durable solutions processes.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

mechanisms.scope: People who are able and eligible to register with UNHCR or a UNHCR-supported registration process may be included in protection and assistance systems.
People who are able and eligible to register with UNHCR or a UNHCR-supported registration process may be included in protection and assistance systems.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when UNHCR records do not interoperate with national civil registration, health, education, social protection, labour, or immigration systems, or when host states do not recognize UNHCR documentation for access.

politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Registration and linked assistance are externally financed and can remain parallel.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: UNHCR registration makes people visible for protection and assistance but does not automatically create domestic legal status or public-system inclusion.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People who cannot access registration, fear registration, lack documentation, are outside operational areas, or fall outside UNHCR's mandate or prioritization may remain invisible.

analysis.oneLineFinding: UNHCR registration can make displaced people visible for protection and assistance, but it does not automatically create domestic legal status, public service access, or fiscal responsibility.

analysis.excluded: People who cannot access registration, fear registration, lack documentation, are outside operational areas, or fall outside UNHCR's mandate or prioritization may remain invisible.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when UNHCR records do not interoperate with national civil registration, health, education, social protection, labour, or immigration systems, or when host states do not recognize UNHCR documentation for access.
analysis.whyItMatters: This is a foundational layer for displacement governance because data inclusion is often mistaken for system inclusion. The political economy archetype is humanitarian legibility without legal absorption.

cases.title: Humanitarian Cash Transfer Systems as Parallel Social Protection

policyRecord.whatItIs: Humanitarian cash transfer systems provide cash or voucher assistance to crisis-affected populations, including refugees and IDPs. They often use humanitarian targeting, payment providers, beneficiary databases, complaints systems, and donor-funded programme cycles.
responsibleInstitutions: Humanitarian agencies, donors, cash working groups, governments, social protection ministries, payment providers, financial institutions, and local authorities may all shape delivery.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on humanitarian targeting, vulnerability criteria, displacement status, registration, documentation, location, and programme resources.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on humanitarian appeals, donor grants, pooled funds, emergency budgets, and sometimes links to social protection or disaster risk financing.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Beneficiary databases, vulnerability assessments, payment records, deduplication systems, complaints data, social registries, and identity systems may be involved.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through humanitarian partners, digital payments, mobile money, banks, prepaid cards, cash-in-hand mechanisms, retailers, and financial service providers.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on whether payments, identity, registration, and eligibility follow people across locations, borders, camps, or status changes.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on complaints and feedback mechanisms, donor reporting, data protection, post-distribution monitoring, targeting transparency, and safeguarding.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Usually short- to medium-term humanitarian programme cycles, with potential transition into longer-term social protection alignment.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

institutionalInterfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Displaced and crisis-affected households selected through humanitarian vulnerability criteria may receive assistance, sometimes alongside host communities.
Displaced and crisis-affected households selected through humanitarian vulnerability criteria may receive assistance, sometimes alongside host communities.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when humanitarian cash ends before households are connected to national social protection, livelihoods, identity systems, financial inclusion, or recurrent public support.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when humanitarian cash ends before households are connected to national social protection, livelihoods, identity systems, financial inclusion, or recurrent public support.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Humanitarian vulnerability criteria create a separate caseload from ordinary social protection.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Humanitarian agencies retain delivery responsibility unless an explicit transition is designed.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Short-term external finance permits coverage without creating recurrent state liability.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Humanitarian cash can stabilize households quickly, but without alignment to national systems it remains a parallel residual rather than sustainable inclusion.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People outside humanitarian targeting, people without registration, households in inaccessible areas, people excluded by documentation or payment requirements, and households whose needs persist after programme closure may be left out.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Humanitarian cash can stabilize displaced households quickly, but without alignment to national systems it can remain a parallel residual rather than a bridge to sustainable inclusion.
analysis.governanceFunction: Humanitarian cash converts emergency need into flexible household support. Its governance importance lies in whether cash systems remain parallel or align with national social protection registries, payment rails, shock-response protocols, and grievance mechanisms.
analysis.included: Displaced and crisis-affected households selected through humanitarian vulnerability criteria may receive assistance, sometimes alongside host communities.
analysis.excluded: People outside humanitarian targeting, people without registration, households in inaccessible areas, people excluded by documentation or payment requirements, and households whose needs persist after programme closure may be left out.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when humanitarian cash ends before households are connected to national social protection, livelihoods, identity systems, financial inclusion, or recurrent public support.
analysis.whyItMatters: Humanitarian cash is one of the most practical interfaces between emergency response and social protection. The political economy archetype is parallel assistance with alignment potential: it can either reinforce humanitarian residualism or help build pathways into ordinary systems.
