cases.title: ADB Safeguard Policy Statement and Environmental and Social Framework

policyRecord.whatItIs: ADB's safeguard architecture, including the 2009 Safeguard Policy Statement and the newer Environmental and Social Framework, sets environmental and social requirements for ADB-supported operations. It includes standards relevant to involuntary resettlement, Indigenous Peoples, consultation, risk management, mitigation, and borrower implementation.
responsibleInstitutions: Borrowers and project executing agencies carry implementation responsibility, with ADB due diligence, supervision, disclosure, and compliance oversight.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility is tied to project impact, cut-off dates, affected-person status, land use, livelihood loss, vulnerability, and resettlement planning categories.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is attached to project budgets, borrower commitments, loan or grant conditions, and environmental and social management plans.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Census surveys, socio-economic baselines, asset inventories, resettlement plans, Indigenous Peoples plans, grievance logs, monitoring reports, and project documentation form the core data architecture.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through borrower agencies, project implementation units, contractors, consultants, compensation systems, livelihood restoration programmes, and grievance mechanisms.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is limited unless project records and entitlements connect affected people to durable land, housing, livelihoods, services, and public administrative systems.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes project grievance mechanisms, ADB supervision, disclosure requirements, compliance review, and borrower obligations under financing agreements.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Project lifecycle, with continuing obligations where resettlement and livelihood restoration impacts persist.

displacementTransition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: local government and ordinary public services

mechanisms.scope: People physically or economically displaced by ADB-financed projects, including some people without formal legal title, may be included where they are identified as project-affected persons. Vulnerable groups and Indigenous Peoples may trigger additional safeguards.

People physically or economically displaced by ADB-financed projects, including some people without formal legal title, may be included where they are identified as project-affected persons. Vulnerable groups and Indigenous Peoples may trigger additional safeguards.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when resettlement plans, compensation, livelihood restoration, or grievance mechanisms remain project-bounded and do not connect affected people to land administration, housing, social protection, civil registration, or local development systems.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when resettlement plans, compensation, livelihood restoration, or grievance mechanisms remain project-bounded and do not connect affected people to land administration, housing, social protection, civil registration, or local development systems.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility is tied to project impact and safeguard classifications.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Borrower and project obligations are supervised through the financing relationship.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Mitigation is financed within project envelopes and does not automatically persist after closure.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Development-induced displacement is governed through project safeguards, mitigation plans, borrower obligations, and lender supervision.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People affected indirectly, cumulatively, outside the project area, after project closure, or through broader market and territorial effects may be harder to capture unless the assessment and mitigation design include them.

analysis.oneLineFinding: ADB safeguards govern displacement within financed projects, but continuity depends on whether resettlement and livelihood restoration connect to ordinary public systems after project implementation.
analysis.governanceFunction: The framework translates development finance into obligations for risk identification, impact assessment, compensation, consultation, resettlement planning, livelihood restoration, grievance redress, and monitoring. In displacement terms, it governs project-created displacement as an environmental and social risk attached to finance.
analysis.included: People physically or economically displaced by ADB-financed projects, including some people without formal legal title, may be included where they are identified as project-affected persons. Vulnerable groups and Indigenous Peoples may trigger additional safeguards.
analysis.excluded: People affected indirectly, cumulatively, outside the project area, after project closure, or through broader market and territorial effects may be harder to capture unless the assessment and mitigation design include them.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when resettlement plans, compensation, livelihood restoration, or grievance mechanisms remain project-bounded and do not connect affected people to land administration, housing, social protection, civil registration, or local development systems.
analysis.whyItMatters: ADB's framework is crucial for Southeast Asia because major infrastructure and development projects often generate displacement risk. The political economy archetype is safeguard-based mitigation: displacement is governed as a project compliance issue, not automatically as a long-term inclusion responsibility.

relatedResearch: The Displacement Continuum and Sustainable Inclusion

versions.continuityEffect: ADB safeguards govern displacement within financed projects, but continuity depends on whether resettlement and livelihood restoration connect to ordinary public systems after project implementation.

cases.title: World Bank IDA Window for Host Communities and Refugees

policyRecord.whatItIs: The World Bank IDA Window for Host Communities and Refugees provides dedicated concessional financing to eligible low-income countries hosting significant refugee populations. It supports medium- to long-term development opportunities for refugees and host communities.
responsibleInstitutions: Host governments, World Bank teams, IDA governance structures, implementing agencies, local authorities, and partner institutions share responsibility through project preparation and implementation.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on country access to IDA, refugee-hosting context, policy commitments, project criteria, and World Bank operational requirements.
policyRecord.financing: Financing is concessional IDA financing dedicated to refugee-hosting contexts, usually through country operations and project-specific allocations.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Refugee registration, national statistics, project beneficiary data, service delivery records, local government data, and monitoring frameworks shape implementation.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through national ministries, local governments, development projects, service providers, livelihood programmes, and infrastructure investments.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on whether project benefits connect refugees to national systems, documentation, labour markets, social protection, and services beyond project areas.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes World Bank project supervision, government implementation responsibilities, grievance mechanisms, results frameworks, and donor/shareholder oversight.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Medium- to long-term development financing, though project cycles may still be shorter than displacement duration.

displacementTransition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

mechanisms.scope: Refugees and host communities in eligible IDA countries may benefit through projects that support services, livelihoods, local development, institutional capacity, or policy reforms.
Refugees and host communities in eligible IDA countries may benefit through projects that support services, livelihoods, local development, institutional capacity, or policy reforms.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when refugee inclusion is project-based, when host-state policies restrict access to work or services, when local institutions are underfinanced after project closure, or when national systems do not absorb recurrent costs.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when refugee inclusion is project-based, when host-state policies restrict access to work or services, when local institutions are underfinanced after project closure, or when national systems do not absorb recurrent costs.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Eligibility depends on refugee-hosting status, policy commitments and country access to concessional finance.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Finance can strengthen government ownership where national systems accept refugee inclusion.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Concessional resources reduce the marginal cost of absorption but remain externally conditioned.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Refugee-hosting costs are partially absorbed into development finance where host-state policy commitments and concessional financing align.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Refugees in countries outside eligibility criteria, urban refugees not captured in project areas, undocumented populations, IDPs, and non-refugee displaced groups may fall outside unless project design includes them through other channels.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The IDA Window for Host Communities and Refugees helps turn refugee hosting into a development-finance issue, but inclusion depends on host-state policy, project design, and whether financing strengthens ordinary systems.
analysis.governanceFunction: The window shifts part of refugee response from humanitarian appeals into development finance. It supports investments in services, livelihoods, institutions, infrastructure, and policy environments that benefit both refugees and host communities.
analysis.included: Refugees and host communities in eligible IDA countries may benefit through projects that support services, livelihoods, local development, institutional capacity, or policy reforms.
analysis.excluded: Refugees in countries outside eligibility criteria, urban refugees not captured in project areas, undocumented populations, IDPs, and non-refugee displaced groups may fall outside unless project design includes them through other channels.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when refugee inclusion is project-based, when host-state policies restrict access to work or services, when local institutions are underfinanced after project closure, or when national systems do not absorb recurrent costs.
analysis.whyItMatters: The window is central to fiscal architecture because it recognizes that host countries incur development costs when hosting refugees. The political economy archetype is conditional fiscal absorption: development finance can make inclusion more feasible, but only where host-state policy and project systems align.

cases.title: Global Concessional Financing Facility

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Global Concessional Financing Facility supports middle-income countries impacted by refugee influxes by using donor contributions to reduce the cost of multilateral development bank loans for projects benefiting refugees and host communities.
responsibleInstitutions: Host governments borrow and implement, partner multilateral development banks prepare and supervise projects, donors provide concessionality, and the World Bank hosts the facility secretariat.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on country income category, refugee-hosting impact, facility rules, MDB project preparation, and donor or steering committee approval.
policyRecord.financing: Financing combines MDB loans with donor-funded grants or concessionality that reduces borrowing costs for eligible projects.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Project data, refugee and host population estimates, development indicators, service delivery data, and financing records shape allocation and monitoring.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through MDB-supported projects implemented by national agencies, local authorities, service providers, and infrastructure or social sector programmes.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on whether project investments are tied to specific localities or connect refugees to broader national systems and rights.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes MDB safeguards, project supervision, government obligations, facility governance, donor oversight, and grievance mechanisms.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Medium-term project and financing cycles, with relevance to protracted refugee situations.

displacementTransition.trigger: project completion or safeguard closure

mechanisms.scope: Refugees and host communities may benefit where eligible middle-income host countries use GCFF-supported financing for services, infrastructure, livelihoods, health, education, or local development.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when projects lower short-term financing costs but do not create durable budget lines, legal access, service entitlements, or institutional responsibility after the project ends.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when projects lower short-term financing costs but do not create durable budget lines, legal access, service entitlements, or institutional responsibility after the project ends.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Refugee-hosting middle-income countries qualify through crisis and facility criteria rather than permanent entitlement.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Financing supports host-government programmes but does not itself assign sustained sectoral responsibility.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Subsidized borrowing lowers financing costs while preserving sovereign debt liability.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Donor-funded concessionality lowers borrowing costs for refugee-hosting states but still works through sovereign loans and project logic.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Countries outside eligibility, displaced populations not framed as refugees, undocumented populations, IDPs, and people outside project areas may remain outside the facility's reach.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The GCFF lowers the financing cost of refugee-hosting development projects, but it still operates through sovereign borrowing and project logic rather than automatic inclusion entitlements.
analysis.governanceFunction: The facility converts refugee hosting into a concessional finance problem. It recognizes that middle-income countries may face major refugee-related costs but lack access to the same concessional terms available to low-income countries.
analysis.included: Refugees and host communities may benefit where eligible middle-income host countries use GCFF-supported financing for services, infrastructure, livelihoods, health, education, or local development.
analysis.excluded: Countries outside eligibility, displaced populations not framed as refugees, undocumented populations, IDPs, and people outside project areas may remain outside the facility's reach.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when projects lower short-term financing costs but do not create durable budget lines, legal access, service entitlements, or institutional responsibility after the project ends.
analysis.whyItMatters: The GCFF is a key instrument for the fiscal architecture of displacement because it addresses the mismatch between refugee-hosting costs and middle-income countries' financing terms. The political economy archetype is subsidized sovereign borrowing for refugee-hosting externalities.

cases.title: OECD Social Protection for the Forcibly Displaced

populationGroups: Forcibly displaced people in low- and middle-income countries

policyRecord.whatItIs: The OECD/EBA paper Social Protection for the Forcibly Displaced in Low- and Middle-Income Countries provides an overview of efforts to extend national social protection systems to forcibly displaced populations. It distinguishes de jure and de facto access and examines enabling conditions for inclusion.
responsibleInstitutions: Host governments, donors, social protection ministries, humanitarian agencies, development partners, UN agencies, and local authorities all shape implementation.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on legal status, documentation, programme rules, poverty or vulnerability criteria, registration systems, and national policy choices.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on domestic social protection budgets, donor support, humanitarian-development alignment, and multi-year financing for inclusion.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Social registries, refugee and IDP registration systems, national ID systems, household surveys, beneficiary databases, and interoperability arrangements are central.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery may run through state social assistance programmes, social insurance schemes, humanitarian cash systems, aligned payment systems, or hybrid approaches.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on whether benefits, registration, identity, and programme records follow displaced people across jurisdictions and status changes.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on programme grievance systems, legal rights, administrative review, donor monitoring, and inclusion of displaced people in national data systems.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Medium- to long-term inclusion agenda, especially relevant to protracted displacement.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

institutionalInterfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: The framework covers refugees and IDPs in low- and middle-income countries, with attention to host communities and social protection systems that may serve both displaced and non-displaced populations.

The framework covers refugees and IDPs in low- and middle-income countries, with attention to host communities and social protection systems that may serve both displaced and non-displaced populations.

The framework covers refugees and IDPs in low- and middle-income countries, with attention to host communities and social protection systems that may serve both displaced and non-displaced populations.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks between legal eligibility and actual access, between humanitarian assistance and state systems, between refugee/IDP databases and citizen registries, and between short-term financing and recurrent entitlements.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks between legal eligibility and actual access, between humanitarian assistance and state systems, between refugee/IDP databases and citizen registries, and between short-term financing and recurrent entitlements.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: The framework distinguishes de jure inclusion from actual access for refugees and IDPs.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: It diagnoses the institutions required for inclusion but is not itself an implementing mandate.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: It identifies sustainable financing as a condition rather than creating a financing mechanism.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Legal or policy access to social protection may exist, but actual inclusion depends on documentation, data, financing, delivery capacity, and politics.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People may remain outside where social protection systems are nascent, underfinanced, citizenship-tied, documentation-dependent, or not designed for mobile and displaced populations.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: De jure inclusion with de facto exclusion

analysis.oneLineFinding: The OECD/EBA framework shows that legal access to social protection often exists on paper, while actual inclusion depends on data, financing, administrative capacity, and political economy.
analysis.governanceFunction: The paper functions as an analytical policy framework for inclusion. It connects forced displacement to social protection architecture, showing that access depends on law, delivery systems, registration, financing, host-state capacity, and donor support.
analysis.included: The framework covers refugees and IDPs in low- and middle-income countries, with attention to host communities and social protection systems that may serve both displaced and non-displaced populations.
analysis.excluded: People may remain outside where social protection systems are nascent, underfinanced, citizenship-tied, documentation-dependent, or not designed for mobile and displaced populations.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks between legal eligibility and actual access, between humanitarian assistance and state systems, between refugee/IDP databases and citizen registries, and between short-term financing and recurrent entitlements.
analysis.whyItMatters: This is one of the clearest analytical bridges between forced displacement and ordinary social protection systems. The political economy archetype is de jure inclusion with de facto exclusion: policy may allow access, but administrative, fiscal, and political barriers determine whether people actually receive support.

relatedResearch: The Displacement Continuum and Sustainable Inclusion

versions.continuityEffect: The OECD/EBA framework shows that legal access to social protection often exists on paper, while actual inclusion depends on data, financing, administrative capacity, and political economy.

cases.title: World Bank Adaptive Social Protection Framework

populationGroups: Displaced people when included in social protection systems

policyRecord.whatItIs: The World Bank adaptive social protection framework focuses on building social protection systems that can help poor and vulnerable households prepare for, cope with, and adapt to shocks. It emphasizes programmes, data and information, finance, institutions, and partnerships.
responsibleInstitutions: Social protection ministries, finance ministries, disaster management agencies, local governments, development partners, and payment or data providers share implementation responsibilities.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on programme rules, poverty or vulnerability criteria, registry inclusion, shock triggers, geographic targeting, and contingency protocols.
policyRecord.financing: Financing may include regular social protection budgets, contingency funds, disaster risk finance, donor support, emergency reallocations, and pre-arranged finance.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Social registries, beneficiary databases, early warning systems, disaster risk data, payment systems, civil registration, and household surveys are central.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through cash transfers, public works, social assistance, social insurance links, payment systems, local administrators, and grievance mechanisms.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on whether benefits and records move with people across districts, borders, displacement sites, and administrative categories.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on programme grievance systems, transparency of targeting, shock-response protocols, audit, and monitoring of inclusion outcomes.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Preparedness, anticipatory action, emergency scale-up, recovery, and long-term resilience.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

institutionalInterfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: Poor and vulnerable households covered by social protection systems may be included, especially where registries, payment systems, contingency finance, and shock-response protocols exist.
Poor and vulnerable households covered by social protection systems may be included, especially where registries, payment systems, contingency finance, and shock-response protocols exist.
Poor and vulnerable households covered by social protection systems may be included, especially where registries, payment systems, contingency finance, and shock-response protocols exist.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when shock response expands vertically or horizontally for registered households but does not capture displaced people who have moved, lost documents, crossed borders, or fallen outside standard poverty targeting.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when shock response expands vertically or horizontally for registered households but does not capture displaced people who have moved, lost documents, crossed borders, or fallen outside standard poverty targeting.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Shock-affected households can be included when registries and programme rules recognize them.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Social-protection institutions retain control while disaster and finance actors provide triggers and resources.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Contingent finance enables expansion but may not sustain post-displacement needs.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Social protection can scale during shocks, but displaced people benefit only if registries, financing, triggers, and payment systems can reach them.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: Displaced households, refugees, informal migrants, undocumented people, and people outside registry systems may remain excluded unless adaptive systems are explicitly designed to include them.

analysis.oneLineFinding: Adaptive social protection strengthens systems for shocks, but displacement inclusion depends on whether registries, financing, and delivery rules can follow people across place and status.
analysis.governanceFunction: The framework links social protection to resilience, disaster risk management, climate adaptation, and crisis response. For displacement governance, its function is to show how ordinary systems can scale, adjust, and respond before humanitarian need becomes entrenched.
analysis.included: Poor and vulnerable households covered by social protection systems may be included, especially where registries, payment systems, contingency finance, and shock-response protocols exist.
analysis.excluded: Displaced households, refugees, informal migrants, undocumented people, and people outside registry systems may remain excluded unless adaptive systems are explicitly designed to include them.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when shock response expands vertically or horizontally for registered households but does not capture displaced people who have moved, lost documents, crossed borders, or fallen outside standard poverty targeting.
analysis.whyItMatters: Adaptive social protection is essential for the displacement continuum because it addresses shocks before, during, and after crisis. The political economy archetype is shock-responsive expansion with registry dependency: systems can scale, but only for people they can see and finance.

cases.title: Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015-2030 is the global framework for reducing disaster risk. It sets priorities on understanding risk, strengthening disaster risk governance, investing in resilience, and enhancing preparedness to build back better in recovery, rehabilitation, and reconstruction.
responsibleInstitutions: States hold primary responsibility, supported by local governments, disaster management agencies, UNDRR, civil society, the private sector, and international cooperation.
policyRecord.eligibility: The framework does not create individual eligibility. Inclusion depends on national DRR policies, local plans, risk assessments, evacuation systems, and recovery programmes.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on national DRR budgets, local government finance, resilience investment, disaster risk finance, development cooperation, and private investment.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Risk assessments, loss and damage databases, early warning systems, exposure data, vulnerability analysis, national statistics, and displacement data shape implementation.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery occurs through national and local DRR systems, preparedness programmes, early warning, evacuation, recovery, reconstruction, infrastructure, and social services.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is weak unless DRR and recovery systems track people after movement and maintain access to assistance, documentation, housing, and services.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is mainly policy and reporting-based, through national strategies, Sendai monitoring, public administration, local governance, and political oversight.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: 2015-2030 global framework spanning prevention, preparedness, response, recovery, rehabilitation, and reconstruction.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: Communities and households exposed to disaster risk, disaster-affected populations, vulnerable groups, and people affected by recovery and reconstruction processes may be included through national DRR strategies.

Communities and households exposed to disaster risk, disaster-affected populations, vulnerable groups, and people affected by recovery and reconstruction processes may be included through national DRR strategies.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when risk data do not identify displacement risk, when evacuation does not connect to recovery, or when build-back-better processes fail to restore housing, livelihoods, documentation, and local services.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when risk data do not identify displacement risk, when evacuation does not connect to recovery, or when build-back-better processes fail to restore housing, livelihoods, documentation, and local services.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Populations at risk are framed through disaster risk rather than durable displacement status.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: Implementation depends on domestic disaster-risk institutions and plans.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: The framework promotes investment in risk reduction but creates no direct fiscal entitlement.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Displacement is addressed upstream through risk reduction, preparedness, resilience, and recovery planning rather than individual entitlement.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People displaced beyond the emergency phase, cross-border disaster-displaced people, people outside local plans, informal settlers, and mobile populations may be left out if DRR systems do not explicitly address displacement.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Sendai Framework strengthens disaster risk governance, but displacement protection depends on whether risk reduction, evacuation, recovery, and social systems are designed around people who move.
analysis.governanceFunction: The framework shifts disaster policy from response toward prevention, risk governance, resilience investment, and recovery. In displacement governance, it provides the prevention and risk-reduction architecture that can reduce disaster displacement and shape post-disaster recovery.
analysis.included: Communities and households exposed to disaster risk, disaster-affected populations, vulnerable groups, and people affected by recovery and reconstruction processes may be included through national DRR strategies.
analysis.excluded: People displaced beyond the emergency phase, cross-border disaster-displaced people, people outside local plans, informal settlers, and mobile populations may be left out if DRR systems do not explicitly address displacement.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when risk data do not identify displacement risk, when evacuation does not connect to recovery, or when build-back-better processes fail to restore housing, livelihoods, documentation, and local services.
analysis.whyItMatters: The Sendai Framework is essential for displacement governance because it situates displacement before the crisis: in exposure, vulnerability, land use, preparedness, and recovery planning. The political economy archetype is prevention-oriented risk governance with weak individual entitlement.

cases.title: Platform on Disaster Displacement and Nansen Protection Agenda

populationGroups: People displaced across borders in disaster contexts
People at risk of disaster displacement
Migrants in climate and disaster contexts

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Nansen Initiative produced the Protection Agenda for people displaced across borders in the context of disasters and climate change. The Platform on Disaster Displacement was created to follow up and support implementation of that agenda.
responsibleInstitutions: States remain central, supported by the Platform on Disaster Displacement, IOM, UNHCR, regional organizations, disaster risk actors, migration authorities, and civil society.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on national or regional tools such as humanitarian admission, temporary stay, visas, migration pathways, evacuation, planned relocation, or protection against return.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on national budgets, international cooperation, climate and disaster risk finance, development assistance, and humanitarian support.
policyRecord.dataSystems: Disaster displacement data, climate risk assessments, migration data, border records, early warning systems, and humanitarian assessments shape policy use.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery may occur through immigration authorities, disaster agencies, consular systems, humanitarian actors, planned relocation programmes, and local services.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is fragile because protection often depends on discretionary status, temporary stay, documentation, and national admission rules.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability is mainly policy-based and cooperative rather than treaty-based, unless domestic or regional law creates specific rights.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Preparedness, displacement response, temporary stay, planned relocation, and longer-term adaptation.

displacementTransition.trigger: status reclassification

institutionalInterfaces.toSystem: recovery, local government or social protection system

mechanisms.scope: People displaced across borders by sudden-onset disasters, slow-onset processes, and climate-related hazards may be addressed through the agenda, as may people at risk of displacement.
People displaced across borders by sudden-onset disasters, slow-onset processes, and climate-related hazards may be addressed through the agenda, as may people at risk of displacement.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when admission is temporary, when protection tools are discretionary, when return is unsafe, or when people move from disaster response into irregular migration status.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when admission is temporary, when protection tools are discretionary, when return is unsafe, or when people move from disaster response into irregular migration status.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: People displaced across borders by disasters are recognized through a policy toolkit without binding status.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: State-led cooperation supports practice development but leaves implementation voluntary.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No standing financing obligation is established.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: States can use migration, humanitarian, disaster, and protection tools for cross-border disaster displacement, but no binding status category is created.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People may remain outside if states do not use discretionary admission, humanitarian visas, temporary protection, migration pathways, or other mechanisms. Internally displaced disaster-affected populations are addressed mainly through other frameworks.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Nansen Protection Agenda and Platform on Disaster Displacement address cross-border disaster displacement, but protection depends on states using migration, humanitarian, and protection tools without a dedicated binding status.
analysis.governanceFunction: The agenda and platform identify practices and policy tools for admission, stay, planned relocation, disaster risk reduction, migration pathways, and protection cooperation in disaster displacement contexts.
analysis.included: People displaced across borders by sudden-onset disasters, slow-onset processes, and climate-related hazards may be addressed through the agenda, as may people at risk of displacement.
analysis.excluded: People may remain outside if states do not use discretionary admission, humanitarian visas, temporary protection, migration pathways, or other mechanisms. Internally displaced disaster-affected populations are addressed mainly through other frameworks.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when admission is temporary, when protection tools are discretionary, when return is unsafe, or when people move from disaster response into irregular migration status.
analysis.whyItMatters: This entry captures one of the main gaps in the displacement continuum: people displaced across borders by disasters often do not fit refugee law but still require protection. The political economy archetype is tool-based protection without a binding status category.

cases.title: IASC Framework on Durable Solutions for Internally Displaced Persons

policyRecord.whatItIs: The IASC Framework on Durable Solutions for Internally Displaced Persons provides criteria and guidance for determining when IDPs have achieved a durable solution through sustainable return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere.
responsibleInstitutions: National authorities carry primary responsibility, supported by humanitarian, development, peacebuilding, human rights, and local government actors.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on internal displacement status and the pursuit of one of the three durable solutions, but durable outcomes are assessed through conditions rather than a single status label.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on national recovery budgets, local development finance, humanitarian-development cooperation, donor support, and sectoral programmes.
policyRecord.dataSystems: IDP profiling, intentions surveys, return monitoring, housing and land records, service access data, livelihood assessments, and local governance data are central.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery occurs through government recovery programmes, local authorities, humanitarian and development partners, housing/land systems, livelihood programmes, and service providers.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is essential: documentation, entitlements, assistance records, school records, health access, and land claims must follow IDPs across return, integration, or settlement locations.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability depends on national law, local governance, participation mechanisms, grievance systems, protection monitoring, and inter-agency coordination.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Medium- to long-term solutions process, often extending well beyond emergency response.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

mechanisms.scope: IDPs pursuing return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, including those in protracted displacement and post-conflict or post-disaster settings.
IDPs pursuing return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, including those in protracted displacement and post-conflict or post-disaster settings.
IDPs pursuing return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, including those in protracted displacement and post-conflict or post-disaster settings.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when IDPs leave emergency assistance but do not regain housing, land, livelihoods, documentation, security, education, health access, or participation in local governance.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when IDPs leave emergency assistance but do not regain housing, land, livelihoods, documentation, security, education, health access, or participation in local governance.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: IDPs remain within the solutions framework until displacement-related needs and discrimination are resolved.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: The framework supplies criteria but depends on national and operational actors for implementation.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: No budget authority accompanies the criteria.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: The framework defines what durable solutions require but relies on governments and partners to finance and implement them.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People may remain outside if governments define solutions narrowly as return, if IDPs are not consulted, if informal settlers lack tenure, or if people are no longer counted after leaving camps.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The IASC Durable Solutions Framework defines when displacement-related needs have ended, but implementation depends on whether governments and partners connect solutions to land, services, livelihoods, safety, and participation.
analysis.governanceFunction: The framework operationalizes durable solutions by identifying conditions such as safety, adequate standard of living, livelihoods, housing, documentation, family reunification, participation, and access to remedies.
analysis.included: IDPs pursuing return, local integration, or settlement elsewhere, including those in protracted displacement and post-conflict or post-disaster settings.
analysis.excluded: People may remain outside if governments define solutions narrowly as return, if IDPs are not consulted, if informal settlers lack tenure, or if people are no longer counted after leaving camps.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when IDPs leave emergency assistance but do not regain housing, land, livelihoods, documentation, security, education, health access, or participation in local governance.
analysis.whyItMatters: The framework is central to the displacement continuum because it defines solutions as more than physical movement. The political economy archetype is solutions criteria without automatic implementation authority.

cases.title: Kampala Convention

populationGroups: People at risk of internal displacement

policyRecord.whatItIs: The African Union Convention for the Protection and Assistance of Internally Displaced Persons in Africa, known as the Kampala Convention, is a binding regional treaty on internal displacement. It covers prevention, protection, assistance, obligations of states and armed groups, and durable solutions.
responsibleInstitutions: States parties carry primary obligations. The African Union, regional bodies, national institutions, armed groups, humanitarian actors, and civil society have supporting or related roles.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on internal displacement status, domestic recognition, treaty incorporation, and national implementation arrangements.
policyRecord.financing: The convention creates obligations but does not itself create a dedicated financing mechanism. Financing depends on national budgets, regional support, donor assistance, and humanitarian or development finance.
policyRecord.dataSystems: IDP registries, national statistics, humanitarian assessments, disaster and conflict data, and monitoring systems shape implementation.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery depends on national and local institutions, humanitarian agencies, social services, disaster systems, protection actors, and durable solutions programmes.
policyRecord.portability: Portability depends on whether IDP documentation, assistance, land claims, civil registration, and service access follow people across displacement, return, relocation, or local integration.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability may include domestic courts, administrative remedies, African Union mechanisms, human rights bodies, political monitoring, and civil society advocacy.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Full displacement cycle: prevention, protection during displacement, assistance, durable solutions, and recovery.

displacementTransition.trigger: expiry of emergency phase or declaration

mechanisms.scope: Internally displaced persons in states parties, people at risk of displacement, and people seeking return, local integration, or relocation may be covered by the convention's obligations.
Internally displaced persons in states parties, people at risk of displacement, and people seeking return, local integration, or relocation may be covered by the convention's obligations.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when treaty obligations are not incorporated into national law, when institutions lack budgets, when local authorities lack capacity, or when IDPs cannot access remedies and services.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when treaty obligations are not incorporated into national law, when institutions lack budgets, when local authorities lack capacity, or when IDPs cannot access remedies and services.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: A binding regional treaty recognizes IDPs across conflict, disaster and development-related contexts.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: States assume legal responsibility but implementation depends on domestic institutions.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: The treaty does not itself ensure funded implementation.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Regional law creates binding IDP obligations, but outcomes depend on domestic incorporation, institutions, budgets, and accountability.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People in non-party states, cross-border refugees, and people whose displacement is not recognized or recorded by domestic systems may remain outside effective protection.

analysis.oneLineFinding: The Kampala Convention is the strongest regional legal instrument on internal displacement, but its impact depends on domestic incorporation, financing, institutions, and implementation.
analysis.governanceFunction: The convention transforms IDP protection from soft-law guidance into binding regional legal obligation for states parties. It addresses conflict, disasters, development projects, and other causes of internal displacement.
analysis.included: Internally displaced persons in states parties, people at risk of displacement, and people seeking return, local integration, or relocation may be covered by the convention's obligations.
analysis.excluded: People in non-party states, cross-border refugees, and people whose displacement is not recognized or recorded by domestic systems may remain outside effective protection.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when treaty obligations are not incorporated into national law, when institutions lack budgets, when local authorities lack capacity, or when IDPs cannot access remedies and services.
analysis.whyItMatters: The Kampala Convention is a benchmark for legal architecture on internal displacement. It shows what stronger legal responsibility can look like, but also why law alone is insufficient without fiscal and administrative absorption. The political economy archetype is binding responsibility with implementation dependency.

cases.title: Colombia Registro Único de Víctimas

populationGroups: Households eligible for assistance and reparations

policyRecord.whatItIs: The Registro Único de Víctimas is Colombia's official registry of people recognized as victims of the armed conflict. It functions as a gateway to humanitarian assistance, compensation, reparations, and public programmes administered through the Victims Unit and related institutions.
responsibleInstitutions: The Victims Unit, national government, local authorities, reparations institutions, land restitution bodies, courts, and social sector agencies share implementation responsibilities.
policyRecord.eligibility: Eligibility depends on victim status, declaration, verification, conflict-related harm, displacement history, household records, and administrative decision-making.
policyRecord.financing: Financing depends on national budget allocations, victim assistance and reparations funds, social sector budgets, local implementation capacity, and long-term fiscal commitments.
policyRecord.dataSystems: The RUV, victim assistance platforms, identity records, compensation systems, land restitution records, social programme databases, and local records shape inclusion.
policyRecord.deliverySystem: Delivery runs through the Victims Unit, local service centres, humanitarian assistance, compensation processes, reparations programmes, social services, and land restitution mechanisms.
policyRecord.portability: Portability is potentially strong because registration is national, but actual access depends on local service availability, documentation, and administrative follow-up.
policyRecord.accountability: Accountability includes administrative review, courts, constitutional jurisprudence, victim participation, oversight bodies, audit, and public reporting.
policyRecord.timeHorizon: Long-term transitional justice, assistance, reparations, and durable solutions architecture.

displacementTransition.trigger: change in phase, place or status

institutionalInterfaces.fromSystem: emergency, humanitarian or sectoral eligibility system

mechanisms.scope: People recognized as victims of the armed conflict, including internally displaced persons and households meeting the legal and administrative criteria for registration.
People recognized as victims of the armed conflict, including internally displaced persons and households meeting the legal and administrative criteria for registration.
People recognized as victims of the armed conflict, including internally displaced persons and households meeting the legal and administrative criteria for registration.

continuityAssessment.explanation: Continuity breaks when registration does not translate into timely assistance, compensation, land restitution, livelihoods, housing, psychosocial support, or durable local inclusion.

diagnostic.residualGaps: Continuity breaks when registration does not translate into timely assistance, compensation, land restitution, livelihoods, housing, psychosocial support, or durable local inclusion.

politicalEconomy.membershipLogic: Victim registration creates a rights-bearing administrative category linked to assistance and reparations.
politicalEconomy.mandateLogic: A dedicated institutional architecture connects registration to state programmes.
politicalEconomy.fiscalExposureLogic: Rights-based obligations are recurrent but operate under significant fiscal strain.
politicalEconomy.inclusionRationale: Legal recognition through a national registry creates access to assistance and reparations, but implementation depends on fiscal and administrative capacity.
politicalEconomy.absorptionConstraint: People not registered, people whose claims are rejected, victims of newer or contested violence, people without documentation, and communities facing barriers to declaration or verification may remain outside.
politicalEconomy.authoredPattern: Rights-based registry inclusion under fiscal strain

analysis.oneLineFinding: Colombia's Victims Registry makes conflict displacement administratively visible and links victims to assistance and reparations, but fiscal capacity and implementation delays shape actual inclusion.
analysis.governanceFunction: The registry converts conflict harm and displacement into administrative recognition. It links legal status as a victim to programmes, services, reparations, and state accountability.
analysis.included: People recognized as victims of the armed conflict, including internally displaced persons and households meeting the legal and administrative criteria for registration.
analysis.excluded: People not registered, people whose claims are rejected, victims of newer or contested violence, people without documentation, and communities facing barriers to declaration or verification may remain outside.
analysis.continuityBreak: Continuity breaks when registration does not translate into timely assistance, compensation, land restitution, livelihoods, housing, psychosocial support, or durable local inclusion.
analysis.whyItMatters: Colombia is one of the clearest examples of registry-mediated displacement governance. Recognition creates a pathway to rights and services, but also exposes the gap between legal inclusion and fiscal/administrative delivery. The political economy archetype is rights-based registry inclusion under fiscal strain.

countryAssessments.synthesis: Update (Jun 2026): the Aug 2025 cabinet resolution and Oct 2025 implementation granted work permits to long-staying Myanmar refugees in the nine border camps; the Royal Thai Government began issuing biometric, non-Thai ID cards in Jun 2026 that formally recognise camp residents as "Myanmar Displaced Persons" and register them in the national civil registry. This is the most significant formalisation of camp-based refugees' status since the camps were established in the 1980s, moving a defined cohort from administrative tolerance toward documented status with civil-registry linkage. The categorical fault line narrows for this cohort but does not close: status remains non-citizen, phased by age and employment, and distinct from asylum recognition or the National Screening Mechanism. Non-refoulement practice remains inconsistent, as the Feb 2025 deportation of long-detained Uyghur asylum seekers illustrates.

Disaster governance is comparatively institutionalized through the Ministry of Interior, but internal displacement is not governed through a distinct longitudinal framework. Stateless and border populations are managed through registration instruments that can restrict movement and reset access across provinces.

Update (Jun 2026): civil-registry integration accompanying the new ID cards is designed to enable access to banking, telecommunications, and healthcare for the camp-based cohort, alongside continued HICS coverage. This adds a second, registry-linked absorption channel alongside the existing premium-based HICS model, though it remains bounded to a defined and phased population rather than a general extension of public-system access to all non-Thai displaced persons.

Update (Jun 2026): the cabinet-level work-rights resolution (2025) and the civil-registry ID rollout (2026) were both executive/administrative actions rather than legislative reform, consistent with the existing pattern of sectoral and cabinet-discretionary coordination. The Ministry of Labour (work permits) and Ministry of Interior (civil registration) are now both load-bearing for this cohort's status, alongside the pre-existing screening and health-ministry channels -- broadening, not replacing, the sectoral coordination pattern. No single longitudinal mandate has been created.

Update (Jun 2026): the work-permit model preserves bounded fiscal exposure by tying camp residents' formal status to employment and a defined population (around 80,000 in nine camps, an estimated 42,000 of working age) rather than creating an open-ended entitlement; revenue effects run through payroll and consumption rather than new transfer obligations. This extends, rather than departs from, the bounded-inclusion logic already established through HICS premium/cost-recovery financing.

Update (Jun 2026): the new ID-card system adds biometric registration, QR-code verification, and civil-registry integration for camp-based Myanmar Displaced Persons -- the first instance of a camp population being folded into the national civil registry rather than managed solely through parallel camp/UNHCR documentation. This is a portability-relevant development: registry inclusion is the precondition for the public-system access (banking, telecom, health) the cards are intended to unlock. It does not yet resolve fragmentation across disaster, health, welfare, and migration records more broadly, and rollout is phased by age and employment status.

Infrastructure-induced displacement is less central than in Mekong dam states, but Thailand is affected by cross-border downstream impacts and return dynamics along the Myanmar border. Development finance is not yet systematically connected to displacement absorption.

Update (Jun 2026): in Jan 2026 the government began rolling out Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP), a government-run registration system developed by MIMOS Berhad that is intended to replace UNHCR's registration role outright. The Home Minister has stated DPP will be the sole recognized refugee documentation, with UNHCR's function narrowed to resettlement referrals only. This is a different mechanism from non-recognition: rather than leaving refugees outside any status system, the state is inserting itself as the sole registering authority while still declining to ratify the 1951 Convention or legislate refugee status. DPP cards do not confer legal status or a right to remain; they are framed as bridging documentation pending resettlement, in a context where resettlement departures fell sharply (1,970 in 2025, down from 8,627 in 2024) after US resettlement cuts. Mass enforcement has intensified in parallel (approx. 92,000 irregular-migrant arrests in 2025 vs 12,000 in 2021), including arrests of UNHCR cardholders, so the categorical fault line persists even as its administrative form changes.

Disaster management is organized around national disaster response but does not systematically include non-citizen or undocumented populations. Internal displacement is not a significant recognized policy category.

Social protection programmes are overwhelmingly citizen or lawful-worker based. Refugees can access some health services at foreign-patient rates but are excluded from ordinary assistance, social insurance, and formal labour access.

Update (Jun 2026): the Home Affairs Ministry, via the national applied-research body MIMOS Berhad, has taken direct operational control of refugee registration through DPP, a function previously performed by UNHCR. This deepens immigration-led governance rather than diversifying it: a security- and documentation-control rationale (curbing fake documentation, responding to a National Security Council directive) now sits alongside the pre-existing immigration-enforcement mandate, with no separate protection-mandated institution holding parallel authority. SUHAKAM, Malaysia's human rights commission, has publicly criticized the secrecy of the underlying NSC Directive No. 23 as insufficient and untimely.

Fiscal architecture reinforces exclusion by reserving social assistance and insurance systems for citizens or documented workers. Refugee inclusion is framed as potential fiscal and membership exposure rather than resilience investment.

Update (Jun 2026): DPP collects biometrics into a government-controlled database, replacing the UNHCR-issued refugee card as the operative identification instrument for those registered. This is the first instance of Malaysia building its own state-held refugee identity system rather than relying on (and periodically disregarding) UNHCR documentation -- a shift from 'parallel identification without state effect' toward state-controlled identification without state-conferred status. No published data-protection safeguards, retention limits, or appeal mechanism have been identified for the system as of mid-2026; HRW wrote the Home Affairs Ministry on 21 Apr 2026 requesting clarification and did not receive a reply.
Development-induced displacement is not the central mapped governance problem, though Orang Asli and land-related displacement raise protection questions outside refugee and migration systems.

Indonesia is primarily a labour-sending state but has a formal refugee-handling instrument. Presidential Regulation 125/2016 assigns state responsibilities but does not create integration pathways or resolve local fiscal implementation gaps.

BNPB provides strong emergency coordination, but support is time-bound. When emergency status expires, responsibility shifts to local government budgets, creating gaps for households whose displacement continues.

Indonesia has significant social protection and health-system capacity through PKH, SIKS-NG, and BPJS Kesehatan, including adaptive enrolment in crises. The problem is not system absence but portability and eligibility continuity.
Formal whole-of-government design exists for refugees and disasters, but implementation is fragmented through decentralised fiscal and administrative authority across hundreds of districts.

The fiscal constraint is jurisdictional. Emergency budgets can activate rapidly, but longer-term responsibility must be absorbed by local governments whose budget ceilings do not automatically follow displaced populations.

The e-KTP and SIKS-NG architecture is highly developed but territorially anchored. Disaster databases and social protection registries do not automatically interoperate, so displaced households can be reclassified or excluded at re-enumeration.

Infrastructure and development displacement are present but less central than disaster and data-system cases in the current chapter. The same absorption constraints would apply where project-affected populations require transfer into national systems.

Outward labour migration is governed through robust longitudinal institutions, while inward migration and domestic displacement receive far less portable protection. This contrast exposes the architecture of membership and portability.

Update (Jun 2026): the 2025 Anticipatory Action Law institutionalized pre-disaster planning and financing, a direct legislative response to the temporal drop-off this cell identifies (disaster response strong at activation, weaker on sustained financing into recovery). Whether the law closes the temporal gap or only moves it earlier in the disaster cycle (pre-positioning finance but still lacking sustained post-disaster transfer mechanisms) is not yet clear from public documentation and is worth tracking as implementation data emerges. Marawi compensation and temporary-shelter problems persist eight years on (approx. 8,200 people still displaced as of Mar 2025, paying rent on privately-owned land where shelters were built), confirming the existing characterization of activation without durable absorption rather than displacing it.
4Ps, Listahanan, PhilHealth, DepEd, and DSWD offer strong institutional capacity, but access is interrupted when displaced households cross municipal boundaries or fall between enumeration cycles.

Whole-of-government disaster coordination is formalized, yet recovery responsibility fragments across housing, education, health, welfare, and local government mandates. No single institution carries longitudinal IDP responsibility.

The Philippines demonstrates both fiscal integration and its limits. 4Ps is legislated and budgeted, but devolved fiscal architecture creates portability gaps when displaced populations move across municipalities.
Listahanan provides a strong registry backbone and post-Haiyan data-matching precedent, but enumeration cycles and cross-municipal moves can interrupt benefits for displaced households.

Infrastructure finance is less central than disaster and conflict displacement in the chapter’s Philippines analysis, though reconstruction and housing after Marawi show how projectized recovery can fragment responsibility.

Migration and displacement are shaped by state-produced statelessness, conflict, labour emigration, and parallel post-coup governance. The 1982 Citizenship Law remains the core categorical exclusion mechanism for Rohingya.

Update (Jun 2026): the Mar 2025 magnitude-7.7 earthquake compounded conflict-driven displacement, damaging tens of thousands of houses and nearly 70 health facilities and disrupting agriculture across central Myanmar. OCHA's 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan projects internal displacement rising from approximately 3.6 million to 4 million during 2026, and -- reflecting funding contraction rather than reduced need -- narrowed its operational scope to life-saving and protection activities in conflict- and earthquake-affected townships (about two-thirds of the country) rather than a nationwide caseload. This reinforces rather than changes the existing 'institutional reversal' characterization, but the earthquake and the 2026 scope narrowing are significant, undocumented additions: disaster and conflict displacement are now compounding in the same response plan, and humanitarian financing itself is contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal was funded) at the same time displacement needs are rising.

Pre-coup social protection expansion through MCCT, social pensions, and disability allowances collapsed after 2021. Health, education, and registration systems have fragmented into state, resistance, and humanitarian channels.

Whole-of-government coordination is structurally impossible under competing authorities and conflict. The mapping therefore reads Myanmar less as an absorption case than as a reconstruction requirements case.

Update (Jun 2026): beyond the domestic fiscal collapse this cell already describes, the 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan's narrowed scope is itself a fiscal-architecture data point: international humanitarian financing for Myanmar is contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal was met) at precisely the moment the Mar 2025 earthquake has compounded conflict-driven need. Myanmar functions here as a limit case not only for domestic fiscal embedding but for the proposition -- central to the chapter's broader argument -- that humanitarian financing cannot substitute indefinitely for sovereign fiscal capacity; where both have collapsed simultaneously, no financing architecture currently absorbs the gap.

IDP data are dispersed across humanitarian registration systems, ethnic governance structures, and fragmented state records. No institutionalized transfer mechanism exists into stable national systems.

Infrastructure-related displacement exists, but post-coup conflict and institutional collapse dominate the governance landscape. Development finance entry points are constrained by legitimacy, sanctions, and access.

Update (Jun 2026): the paper hokhau household registration book formally ceased validity on 1 Jan 2023, replaced by an ID-number-linked national population database under the 2020 Law on Residence. Ministry of Public Security officials have stated explicitly that this is a shift in administrative form -- paper to digital -- rather than an end to residence-tied management of access and mobility. The territorial legibility this cell describes therefore persists through the new instrument rather than being resolved by it; references to 'the household registration book' as a live mechanism should be updated to the post-2023 digital system, while the underlying characterization (state-directed legibility over portability) appears to hold.
Disaster and climate adaptation capacity is strong, including planned relocation in the Mekong Delta, but relocation is framed as spatial development rather than displacement protection.

Health, education, poverty targeting, and social insurance are administered through territorially anchored systems. Moving across commune or registration boundaries can disrupt access regardless of underlying vulnerability.

Whole-of-government coherence is strong where it serves state spatial and development objectives. The issue is not weak coordination but the orientation of coordination toward territorial control rather than longitudinal protection.

Fiscal responsibility is tied to local administrative units and state development plans. Resettlement support is often project or programme based rather than embedded as portable long-term entitlement.

Update (Jun 2026): the underlying data architecture for residence-based access shifted in 2023 from paper household registration books to a centralized, ID-number-linked digital population database. This could plausibly be read as a portability improvement (digital records travel more easily than paper books) or as a more efficient version of the same territorial-control logic (the database is still organized around registered residence, and de-registration/re-registration on relocation still appears to govern access). The characterization should specify which reading the evidence supports rather than describing the pre-2023 paper mechanism as if it were still current.

Infrastructure and planned relocation are central. Resettlement compensation exists, but records and support do not consistently integrate with poverty or service systems after households cross administrative boundaries.

The primary mobility issue is labour outmigration to Thailand, while internal displacement is driven largely by land concessions, infrastructure, and dam construction. Refugee law exists but is weakly implemented.

countryAssessments.synthesis: Update (Jun 2026): IDPoor, Cambodia's national poverty/social-protection registry, has moved from a fixed three-year survey cycle to on-demand digital registration -- piloted in 2017, scaled nationally from 2020 under COVID-19 pressure, and now formalized by the 2023 Law on Civil Registration, Vital Statistics, and Identification (CRVSID), with a biometric, QR-verified eID rolling out from 2025. The previous registry-cycle gap (households missing a survey round had to wait years for the next) has been substantially narrowed for IDPoor specifically, though the broader question of cross-system interoperability (IDPoor vs. civil registration vs. other sectoral databases) and whether mobile or displaced households can register on demand at their actual location remains less clear from public documentation. The existing characterization should be checked against whether it is describing this current on-demand architecture or the earlier fixed-cycle system.

countryAssessments.synthesis: Infrastructure displacement is central. Lower Sesan 2 shows how compensation and relocation packages can be provided while service access and vulnerability continue beyond the project boundary.

externalContext.sovereigntySensitivity: Territorial state-directed sovereignty with weaker capacity

countryAssessments.synthesis: Mobility governance is dominated by labour emigration to Thailand and state-directed resettlement of highland communities. Inward refugee governance is limited despite formal Convention accession.

Disaster governance exists but remains weaker than in larger neighbours. The Attapeu dam collapse exposed the interface between disaster response, infrastructure liability, and displaced-community support.
National social protection infrastructure is limited, especially in rural and resettled areas. Health, education, and welfare access often depend on village location and project-specific support.

Whole-of-government coherence is organized around development and infrastructure priorities. Resettlement is handled through project bodies and line ministries rather than a cross-sectoral IDP protection mandate.
Displacement support is usually project financed and bounded by resettlement action plans. Limited ordinary social protection capacity makes fiscal embedding difficult after project closure.
Administrative and social protection data systems are less developed than in Indonesia, Vietnam, or the Philippines. Project records rarely provide a pathway into broader social support.

Hydropower and corridor infrastructure are central. Xayaburi illustrates the mismatch between safeguard-calibrated project obligations and displacement as a longitudinal, cross-border livelihood condition.

vignette.function: Shows why safeguards calibrated to project closure cannot govern downstream and long-term livelihood disruption.

vignette.name: 2025-26 refugee work-permit and biometric ID formalisation
vignette.pattern: Sovereignty-preserving functional access, with phased formalisation for a defined cohort
vignette.function: The Aug 2025 cabinet resolution granted work permits to long-staying Myanmar refugees in the nine border camps (population approx. 80,000-81,000, of whom roughly 42,000 of working age); more than 5,500 had joined the workforce by mid-2026. In Jun 2026 the Royal Thai Government began issuing biometric, QR-verified, non-Thai ID cards recognising recipients as 'Myanmar Displaced Persons' and integrating them into the national civil registry, enabling banking, telecommunications, and healthcare access. Issuance is phased by age (5+) and prioritises those already employed. This is the most significant formalisation of camp-based refugee status since the camps were established in the 1980s, and the first instance of civil-registry integration for this population -- but it is bounded (non-citizen, employment- and age-phased) and distinct from asylum recognition. Non-refoulement practice for other populations (e.g., the Feb 2025 deportation of detained Uyghur asylum seekers) remains inconsistent, underscoring that formalisation is selective rather than systemic.
vignette.sources: UNHCR Thailand, "Government-issued ID cards mark new era of refugee inclusion and self-reliance in Thailand," press release, 17 Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/th/en/government-issued-id-cards-mark-new-era-of-refugee-inclusion%20and%20self-reliance-in-thailand | UNHCR, "UNHCR welcomes Royal Thai Government resolution providing long-term refugees the right to work," press release, 26 Aug 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-welcomes-royal-thai-government-resolution-providing-long-term-refugees | Human Rights Watch, "Thailand Allows Myanmar Refugees in Camps to Work Legally," 27 Aug 2025, https://www.hrw.org/news/2025/08/27/thailand-allows-myanmar-refugees-in-camps-to-work-legally | The Diplomat, "Thai Government Grants Refugees From Myanmar the Right to Work," 27 Aug 2025, https://thediplomat.com/2025/08/thai-government-grants-refugees-from-myanmar-the-right-to-work/

vignette.name: Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP) state-led refugee registration
vignette.function: Beginning Jan 2026, Malaysia rolled out the DPP, a biometric registration system built by MIMOS Berhad (the national applied R&D agency) under Home Affairs Ministry control, explicitly intended to replace UNHCR's role in registering and documenting refugees. The Home Minister told parliament DPP would become the sole recognized refugee documentation, with UNHCR's role narrowed to resettlement referrals. More than 210,000 refugees and asylum seekers were UNHCR-registered as of late 2025 (about 90% from Myanmar, including 120,000+ Rohingya); first-phase rollout targeted Rohingya detainees in immigration detention. DPP cards do not confer legal status, a right to work generally, or protection from arrest; officials describe them as bridging documentation pending resettlement, even as resettlement departures fell sharply (1,970 in 2025 vs 8,627 in 2024) following US resettlement cuts. No published data-protection safeguards or appeal mechanism have been identified, and mass immigration enforcement (approx. 92,000 arrests in 2025) continued throughout the rollout, including arrests of people holding UNHCR documentation. This is a categorical-fault-line case in which the state inserts itself as registering authority while the underlying exclusion from legal status is preserved -- a distinct mechanism from simple non-recognition, worth tracking as it may prefigure similar moves elsewhere in the region.
vignette.sources: Human Rights Watch, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration System Raises Concerns," 4 May 2026, https://www.hrw.org/news/2026/05/04/malaysia-new-refugee-registration-system-raises-concerns | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: Rohingya Refugees Held Indefinitely in Inhumane and Degrading Conditions," 12 Mar 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-03-12/ | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration Scheme Must Protect Rights," 1 Jun 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-06-01/ | UNHCR Malaysia, "The UN Refugee Agency," accessed Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/my/ (confirms DPP launched Jan 2026 as government-led registration system) | New Straits Times, "Refugee policy proposal to be presented to cabinet by mid-June," 19 May 2026, https://www.nst.com.my/amp/news/nation/2026/05/1443346/refugee-policy-proposal-be-presented-cabinet-mid-june

vignette.name: March 2025 earthquake and compounding humanitarian financing contraction
vignette.pattern: Compounding collapse: disaster displacement layered on institutional reversal amid financing contraction
vignette.function: The Mar 2025 magnitude-7.7 earthquake struck central Myanmar, damaging tens of thousands of houses, dozens of roads and bridges, and nearly 70 health facilities, and disrupting agriculture for an estimated 2 million newly affected people. It compounded conflict-driven displacement that had already reached 3.6 million by late 2025; OCHA's 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan projects this rising to approximately 4 million during 2026. Reflecting funding contraction rather than reduced need, the 2026 plan narrowed its scope to life-saving and protection activities in conflict- and earthquake-affected townships (about two-thirds of the country), down from a nationwide caseload, after only 26% of the 2025 appeal was funded. This case illustrates a distinct failure mode from the 2021 institutional-reversal case already in the database: here, disaster and conflict displacement compound each other at the same time the international financing that has substituted for collapsed domestic fiscal capacity is itself receding -- a scenario the chapter's fiscal-architecture argument should anticipate as the limit case within the limit case.
vignette.sources: OCHA, "Myanmar Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan 2026," 10 Dec 2025, https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/myanmar/myanmar-humanitarian-needs-and-response-plan-2026 | World Food Programme, "WFP warns that Myanmar faces rising displacement and unacceptable hunger levels in 2026," https://www.wfp.org/news/wfp-warns-myanmar-faces-rising-displacement-and-unacceptable-hunger-levels-2026
